  "new_string": "    mesh = mesh.clean(tolerance=1e-5)\n    try:\n        mesh = mesh.fill_holes(1000.0).clean(tolerance=1e-5)  # close neck-cut cap\n    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001\n        logger.info(\"%s: fill_holes skipped (%s)\", name, exc)\n    mesh = mesh.triangulate()\n\n    # Marching cubes already lands near the target (see _choose_step_size), so\n    # only decimate if still well over -- and gently, with topology preserved,\n    # because decimation is the step most likely to break manifoldness.\n    n_faces = mesh.n_faces_strict if hasattr(mesh, \"n_faces_strict\") else mesh.n_cells\n    if n_faces > 1.4 * target_triangles:\n        reduction = 1.0 - target_triangles / float(n_faces)\n        mesh = mesh.decimate_pro(reduction, preserve_topology=True,\n                                 feature_angle=45.0)\n        mesh = mesh.clean(tolerance=1e-5).triangulate()\n\n    out_faces = mesh.faces.reshape(-1, 4)[:, 1:].astype(np.int64)\n    out_verts = np.asarray(mesh.points, dtype=np.float64)\n    return _remove_unreferenced(out_verts, out_faces)"

```json
{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\claude\\C--Users-USER-Documents-Henri-Mondor-IRM-to-MNE-python\\6c11610c-cac9-4396-8398-266fd741c3e0\\scratchpad\\proto_mesh_bem.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Prototype: extract nested BEM shells as boundaries of tissue-tet unions.\n\nRuns in simnibs_env (has simnibs.read_msh). Boundary of a tet set = faces used\nby exactly one tet in the set -> a closed manifold surface by construction.\n\"\"\"\nimport sys\nimport numpy as np\nimport simnibs\n\nM2M = sys.argv[1]\nOUT = sys.argv[2]\n\nm = simnibs.read_msh(M2M + \"/sampleE2E.msh\")\ntet = m.elm.elm_type == 4\ntags = m.elm.tag1\ntets = m.elm.node_number_list[tet][:, :4] - 1  # 0-based node indices\ntet_tag = tags[tet]\ncoords = m.nodes.node_coord  # world mm\n\n# The four triangular faces of a tet.\n_FACES = [(0, 1, 2), (0, 1, 3), (0, 2, 3), (1, 2, 3)]\n\n\ndef boundary_surface(tissue_tags):\n    sel = np.isin(tet_tag, tissue_tags)\n    sub = tets[sel]\n    faces = np.concatenate([sub[:, f] for f in _FACES], axis=0)\n    faces_sorted = np.sort(faces, axis=1)\n    # Faces used once within the set are on the boundary.\n    uniq, inv, cnt = np.unique(faces_sorted, axis=0, return_inverse=True,\n                               return_counts=True)\n    boundary_mask = cnt[inv] == 1\n    bfaces = faces[boundary_mask]          # keep original winding\n    used = np.unique(bfaces)\n    remap = np.full(coords.shape[0], -1, np.int64)\n    remap[used] = np.arange(len(used))\n    return coords[used], remap[bfaces]\n\n\ndef euler(rr, tris):\n    e = np.sort(np.vstack([tris[:, [0, 1]], tris[:, [1, 2]], tris[:, [2, 0]]]), axis=1)\n    ue, c = np.unique(e, axis=0, return_counts=True)\n    nonman = int((c != 2).sum())\n    return len(rr) - len(ue) + len(tris), nonman\n\n\nshells = {\n    \"inner_skull\": [1, 2, 3],\n    \"outer_skull\": [1, 2, 3, 7, 8],\n    \"outer_skin\": [1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10],\n}\nc = coords.mean(0)\nfor name, tt in shells.items():\n    rr, tris = boundary_surface(tt)\n    chi, nonman = euler(rr, tris)\n    r = np.linalg.norm(rr - c, axis=1).mean()\n    print(f\"{name:12s}: {len(tris):7d} tris, {len(rr):7d} verts, \"\n          f\"Euler={chi}, non-manifold_edges={nonman}, mean_r={r:.1f} mm\")\n    np.savez(f\"{OUT}/{name}.npz\", rr=rr, tris=tris)\n\n# Coordinate frame sanity: print mesh world extent to compare with T1.\nprint(\"mesh world x:[%.0f,%.0f] y:[%.0f,%.0f] z:[%.0f,%.0f]\" % (\n    coords[:, 0].min(), coords[:, 0].max(), coords[:, 1].min(),\n    coords[:, 1].max(), coords[:, 2].min(), coords[:, 2].max()))\n"
}

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T21:43:29.603Z</sub>

Excellente question, et elle mérite une réponse franche.

## Ce que je fais maintenant est **nécessaire**, pas de l'optimisation

Rien à voir avec le gain de temps (ça, c'était la discussion `--segment`, qu'on a mise de côté). Le problème est de **correction** : ma méthode d'origine — marching cubes sur le volume de labels + décimation — **échoue à produire une surface de crâne fermée valide sur la vraie segmentation `charm`**. Je l'ai constaté noir sur blanc : `outer_skull` reste non-manifold après toutes les tentatives. Tant que ce n'est pas remplacé par quelque chose de fiable, **le pipeline ne va pas jusqu'au bout sur données réelles**. Donc oui, il faut corriger, ce n'est pas cosmétique.

## Mais tu as raison de me reprendre, et voici la nuance honnête

Ce que je prototypais (extraire les coquilles comme bord d'unions de tétraèdres) **s'appuie sur le maillage validé de SimNIBS** — ça, c'est la partie fiable et éprouvée. Mais l'extraction elle-même reste ~30 lignes de mon code numpy. C'est une opération **exacte et déterministe** (pas heuristique comme le marching cubes), donc fiable, mais ce n'est pas littéralement « une fonction SimNIBS ». Tu as le nez creux.

Il y a en réalité **deux vraies voies**, et c'est un choix d'architecture qui t'appartient :

**A. BEM à partir du maillage SimNIBS** — j'extrais 3 coquilles emboîtées du maillage validé, puis je les passe à la machinerie BEM de MNE que j'ai **déjà validée** (fantôme + sample). Code perso modéré (l'extraction), mais tout l'aval est éprouvé.

**B. Leadfield FEM natif de SimNIBS** — on n'extrait **aucune** surface BEM. On utilise le solveur EEG de SimNIBS (FEM), qui est **la** méthode SimNIBS de référence pour l'EEG, documentée avec une intégration MNE officielle. Plus précis pour le crâne (FEM > BEM 3 couches). Mais c'est une refonte plus lourde de l'étape « forward », un chemin différent de ce qu'on a construit.

Mon avis : si tu veux le **plus éprouvé et le plus précis**, c'est **B** (c'est littéralement ce pour quoi SimNIBS existe côté EEG). Si tu veux **réutiliser au maximum ce qui est déjà validé** et rester dans l'écosystème MNE-BEM classique, c'est **A**.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T21:49:00.045Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
The user picked Option B because they need an established method for publication rather than having to justify novel methodology. Before I implement anything, they want me to assess how much this changes the current codebase, so I'm holding off on coding and instead thinking through what integrating FEM leadfield via SimNIBS would actually entail for the existing pipeline.

SimNIBS has a dedicated EEG leadfield workflow: I'd use the head model we already have from the charm folder, define electrode positions on the scalp in subject space, then run SimNIBS's FEM solver to compute how dipole sources in gray matter map to electrode potentials. SimNIBS can export this directly into MNE-Python format through its `simnibs.eeg` module, which handles the conversion to a forward model or source space that MNE expects.

The key difference is that SimNIBS's standard EEG workflow uses a cortical surface source space derived from charm's gray matter surfaces, not the volumetric approach we were considering earlier — though I should verify whether SimNIBS can accommodate a volumetric source space if that's what we need.

For the electrodes, I need to feed the coregistered scalp positions into SimNIBS, which means our ICP coregistration step is still essential to transform the electrode locations into the subject's mesh coordinate system.

The FEM solve itself runs within SimNIBS and can take anywhere from minutes to an hour depending on the mesh resolution and number of electrodes. Once that completes, SimNIBS outputs the leadfield as an MNE-compatible forward model, and from there our existing inverse pipeline—noise covariance, inverse operator, source estimation—all works without modification.

Looking at what changes in the workflow: the DICOM to NIfTI conversion stays the same, and we still run the segmentation, but now we can't use the segment-only flag since we need the full mesh and source space surfaces. The anonymization and EEG reading steps remain unchanged.

What gets removed entirely is the BEM surface extraction logic—all that marching cubes, nested mask handling, and gap checking that was fragile and prone to failure. SimNIBS handles that internally, so we bypass it completely.

The old MNE-based pipeline functions like BEM solution building, volumetric source space creation, and the forward model computation all get replaced by SimNIBS's FEM leadfield approach, which then converts back to an MNE Forward object for compatibility.

On the new side, I'm building a wrapper around SimNIBS's EEG leadfield computation—either calling it as a subprocess in its own environment or importing it directly—that handles source space setup from the cortical mesh, electrode positioning, and running the leadfield calculation.

The refactoring involves retiring most of surfaces.py, stripping BEM/source-space functions from mne_subject.py while keeping T1.mgz building for visualization, completely rewriting forward.py to delegate to SimNIBS, and creating a new simnibs_eeg module to orchestrate the leadfield computation and conversion back to MNE format.

The wrapper and pipeline stages need rewiring to feed into SimNIBS, and surface-related tests get replaced with harder-to-unit-test leadfield integration tests. This is a medium-to-large refactor that actually simplifies things by removing the most fragile module, though the exact SimNIBS EEG API and whether to use cortical or volumetric source spaces remain open questions. I should do a quick check of the SimNIBS API to ground my estimate in what's actually available rather than relying on assumptions.
```
</details>

def compute_tdcs_leadfield(
    m2m_dir: Union[Path, str],

    run_kwargs: Union[dict, None] = None,
):
    """Convenience function for running a TDCS simulation. The result can be
    converted to an EEG forward solution using `make_forward`.

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    m2m_dir : str
    fem_dir : str
    fname_montage : str
        The filename of the montage to use.
    subsampling : int | None
        The subsampling to use. If the files do not exist, they are created

        Whether to model electrodes as points (defaults) or mesh them onto the
        headmodel.

        Kwargs used to update the initialization of `TDCSLEADFIELD`. Can be
        used to override default settings, e.g., by passing another list of
        conductivities which can be achieve by doing
        `init_kwargs=dict(cond=my_conds)` where my_conds has the same format
        as `simnibs.simulation.cond.standard_cond` (default = None).

    fname : PathLike
        The name of the hdf5 file containing the simulation results.

        run_kwargs = dict(save_mat=False, cpus=1)

def make_forward(
    m2m_dir: Union[Path, str],

    trans: Union[None, Path, str] = None,

        The output format of the source, morph, and forward objects. Supported

        Filename or instance of mne.Info (only used [and mandatory] when

            6  ->  40,962
            7  -> 163,842 (full resolution)
        By default, use fsaverage 7. Use None to omit morph.

        Whether or not to write the results to disk. The forward object is

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T21:52:06.601Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
I'm mapping out the SimNIBS EEG FEM workflow and identifying what inputs are needed for the leadfield computation—specifically the m2m directory from CHARM, an output directory for the FEM results, and a montage file with electrode positions in SimNIBS format. ge CSV from coregistered electrodes, then run the FEM solver to generate the leadfield, and finally use make_forward to produce the MNE forward model with fsaverage morphing for group-level analysis. A key realization: this pipeline commits us to cortical surface sources rather than volumetric, which is the standard for SimNIBS/MNE and publishable, but it's a shift from the earlier volumetric approach—I need to flag this to the user. Also, the whole workflow depends on having both simnibs and mne available in the same environment, and we definitely need charm's cortical surfaces (no segment optimization shortcut).

The practical question is whether mne is already in simnibs_env or if we need to install it there. Since make_forward calls mne utilities and needs the m2m and leadfield files, the cleanest approach is to run the entire SimNIBS-to-forward pipeline in simnibs_env, which means adding mne to that environment if it's not already there.

On the code side, this means retiring several modules that were handling the fragile surface generation and MNE BEM setup: surfaces.py with its marching-cubes and mask logic, mne_subject.py with its anatomy and source space functions, and forward.py's make_forward implementation. These are all replaced by the SimNIBS forward approach, which simplifies the codebase significantly.

Most of the pipeline stays intact though — dicom_convert, anonymize, eeg, and inverse all work unchanged since they're either independent or consume the SimNIBS output directly. The headmodel wrapper still needs to run the full charm pipeline, and coregistration still produces the transformation but relies on having a scalp surface for the ICP fitting, which currently comes from the BEM output.

With the FEM route, I still need that scalp surface for coregistration, but instead of extracting three nested BEM shells, I can just pull the skin surface from SimNIBS's mesh — it's much simpler and doesn't need to be a perfect closed manifold since ICP only needs point clouds. So coregistration.py stays but its scalp source switches to the charm skin surface, which MNE's coreg expects as a head.fif file.

The scaffolding modules mostly stay as-is with rewiring for the forward stage. I'm adding a new simnibs_forward.py module that takes coregistered electrodes, converts them to a SimNIBS montage CSV, calls the leadfield computation and forward model generation via subprocess in the SimNIBS environment, then returns the forward solution and source space. I'll also need MNE installed in that environment and a small scalp extraction utility for coregistration.

One key shift: the source space will be cortical (surface-based) rather than volumetric, which I should flag to the user. Fiducials and coregistration are still required.

The forward model ends up in HEAD coordinates with cortical sources. This is a medium-complexity change overall—it actually removes the most fragile parts (surfaces, BEM, volumetric source handling), making the codebase simpler despite adding the SimNIBS wrapper. The net effect is probably fewer lines of code, though the main unknowns are FEM solve time and memory usage at scale.

The trickiest parts will be getting the electrode coregistration into mesh space and ensuring the FEM solve runs smoothly, rather than the MNE integration itself since SimNIBS handles that officially. The reciprocity-based leadfield means one FEM solve per electrode (or source, whichever is fewer), so for 60–256 electrodes on an 800k-node mesh, that's potentially a lot of computation.

Since the user only asked for an estimate first, I should give them a clear impact assessment with the key decision points flagged—cortical source space, compute cost, and the mne-in-simnibs_env requirement—and let them confirm before moving to implementation. I'll outline what's gained (the official MNE-native FEM EEG pipeline), what's removed (the fragile BEM surfaces), and what carries over from their existing setup. I need to confirm the cortical source space approach and note that mne isn't currently in the simnibs environment, so that'll need to be installed as part of the setup. Now I'm ready to write out the estimate with a clear structure.
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T21:52:29.227Z</sub>

J'ai maintenant une vue précise et fondée. Voici l'estimation d'impact avant de toucher au code.

## La bonne nouvelle : c'est une API officielle et MNE-native

SimNIBS 4.6 fournit exactement la chaîne publiable que tu veux, en trois appels :
1. `compute_tdcs_leadfield(m2m_dir, fem_dir, montage)` → résout le leadfield **FEM** (réciprocité TDCS).
2. `make_forward(m2m_dir, leadfield, out_format="mne", info=..., trans=...)` → construit un **objet `mne.Forward`**, met en place l'espace de sources, et **écrit directement un `-fwd.fif`**. Bonus : il fournit aussi un morph vers `fsaverage` pour l'analyse de groupe.
3. Notre moitié inverse consomme ce `-fwd.fif` **sans rien changer**.

Donc l'intégration MNE, c'est SimNIBS qui la fait — pas moi. C'est ça qui rend la méthode citable.

## Ce que ça retire (la partie fragile)

| Module | Sort |
|---|---|
| `surfaces.py` (marching cubes, masques emboîtés, réparation maillage, ~470 l.) | **supprimé** |
| `mne_subject.py` : BEM + source space volumétrique | **remplacé** |
| `forward.py` : forward BEM MNE | **remplacé** par l'appel SimNIBS |
| `test_surfaces.py` | retiré |

Autrement dit, **on supprime précisément le code qui échouait**. C'est une simplification, pas une complexification.

## Ce qui est réutilisé tel quel (les moitiés déjà validées)

DICOM→NIfTI ✅ · `charm` ✅ · anonymisation ✅ · lecture EEG + covariance ✅ · **opérateur inverse + estimation de sources ✅** (il mange n'importe quel `Forward`). La coregistration reste, mais je n'ai plus besoin d'extraire 3 coquilles — **une seule surface de scalp** (depuis le maillage SimNIBS) suffit pour l'ICP, ce qui est bien plus simple et robuste.

## Ce qui est nouveau

Un module `simnibs_forward.py` (~200-250 lignes) : convertir les électrodes coregistrées en montage SimNIBS (.csv), lancer le leadfield FEM + `make_forward` en sous-processus dans `simnibs_env`, récupérer le `-fwd.fif`.

**Bilan taille :** la base de code va probablement **rétrécir** (~−500 lignes retirées, ~+250 ajoutées).

## Trois conséquences à valider avant que je me lance

1. **L'espace de sources devient cortical (surfacique), plus volumétrique.** C'est le standard SimNIBS/FEM et c'est ce qui est publié — mais ça **inverse** ton choix initial du volumétrique. Pour une publi, le surfacique cortical est la norme ; je pense que c'est cohérent avec ta logique « méthode établie ». À confirmer.
2. **Il faut le `charm` complet** (mesh + surfaces) — on abandonne définitivement l'optim `--segment`. Tu l'avais déjà accepté.
3. **Coût de calcul en plus :** le leadfield FEM résout un système par électrode sur un maillage à ~800k nœuds. Pour 60-256 électrodes, ça peut aller de dizaines de minutes à ~1-2 h de plus par sujet. C'est le prix de la précision FEM.

Et un point de **mise en place** : `simnibs_env` n'a pas `mne` (son yml ne l'inclut pas). Il faudra l'y installer pour que `make_forward` sorte du MNE — trivial (`pip install mne` dans cet env).

## En résumé

Changement **moyen**, bien borné, appuyé sur une API officielle. Il retire la partie qui posait problème et garde intactes les moitiés validées. Le vrai travail se concentre sur un seul module (plomberie électrodes→SimNIBS→fif) et la bascule vers un espace de sources cortical.

Veux-tu que je parte là-dessus ? Et confirmes-tu le passage à l'**espace de sources cortical** (le choix cohérent avec une méthode publiable), ou tiens-tu à garder du volumétrique — auquel cas je vérifie d'abord si le leadfield FEM SimNIBS accepte une grille volumique, ce qui est moins standard ?

### 📤 (tool results)  <sub>2026-07-20T21:56:10.628Z</sub>

<details><summary>📤 Tool result</summary>

```text
=== setup_source_space + make_source_space (utils_mne): surfacique uniquement ?
def setup_source_space(
    m2m_dir: Path | str,
    subsampling: int | None = None,
    morph_to_fsaverage: int | None = 5,
):
    """Setup a source space for use with MNE-Python.

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    m2m_dir : Path-like
        The `m2m` directory containing the segmentation and head model results.
    subsampling : None | int
        The subsampling to use (default = None).
    morph_to_fsaverage : int | None
        Whether or not to create a mapping from subject space to the fsaverage
        template (default = 5, which constructs a morph to the fsaverage 10k
        model). If None, no morph will be computed.

    RETURNS
    -------
    src_from : mne.SourceSpaces
        The source space object.
    morph : mne.SourceMorph | None
        The source morph object.
    """
    m2m = SubjectFiles(subpath=str(m2m_dir))
    src_from = load_subject_surfaces(m2m, "central", subsampling)
    # if surface is subsampled, add normals from original surface
    if subsampling:
        for h in src_from:
            src_from[h]["normals"] = np.loadtxt(
                m2m.get_morph_data(h, "normals.csv", subsampling),
                delimiter=",",
            )
    src_from = make_source_spaces(src_from, m2m.subid)

    # Make source morph
    if morph_to_fsaverage:
        subid_to = f"fsaverage{morph_to_fsaverage})"
        src_to = load_fsaverage_surfaces("central", morph_to_fsaverage)
        src_to = make_source_spaces(src_to, subid_to)

        morphs = cross_subject_map(m2m, "fsaverage", subsampling, morph_to_fsaverage)
        mmaps = {h: v.morph_mat for h, v in morphs.items()}
        morph = make_source_morph(src_from, src_to, mmaps)
    else:
        morph = None
    return src_from, morph


def make_source_spaces(
    surf: dict, subject_id: Union[None, str] = None, coord_frame: str = "mri"
):
    """Create an MNE-Python source space object.

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    surf : dict
        Dictionary with entries `lh` and `rh` each being a dictionary with keys
        `points`, `tris`, and possibly `normals`.
    subject_id : str
        The id of the subject.

    RETURNS
    -------
    src : mne.SourceSpaces
        The source space object.
    """
    return mne.SourceSpaces(
        [
            make_source_space(
                **v, coord_frame=coord_frame, surf_id=k, subject_id=subject_id
            )
            for k, v in surf.items()
        ]
    )


def make_source_space(
    points: np.ndarray,
    tris: Union[None, np.ndarray] = None,
    normals: Union[None, np.ndarray] = None,
    coord_frame: str = "mri",
    surf_id: Union[None, str] = None,
    subject_id: Union[None, str] = None,
):
    """Setup a dictionary of a single hemisphere for an MNE-Python source space
    object.

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    points : ndarray
        Source positions (in mm).
    tris : ndarray
        The triangulation of the source positions if relevant (default = None).
    normals : ndarray
        Source normals (default = None).
    coord_frame : str
        The coordinate frame of the source positions. Choose `mri` or `head`
        default = 'mri')
    surf_id : str
```
</details>

Looking deeper, the TDCSLEADFIELD in SimNIBS can actually compute the leadfield across the entire gray matter volume rather than just the cortical surface — you can specify the tissue region and it'll evaluate at volume points. The constraint is that the MNE conversion helper functions may not fully support arbitrary volumetric positions, even if the underlying FEM leadfield does. The key insight is that SimNIBS can technically compute a volumetric leadfield by setting `tissues=[2]` for gray matter volume and leaving interpolation unset, which would give you leadfield values at the tetrahedra element centers rather than on the surface. However, the MNE integration through `make_forward` is hardcoded to use the cortical surface, so accessing a volumetric leadfield would require bypassing that standard pipeline and manually assembling the Forward model from the raw volumetric data—which isn't the documented approach.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:01:00.726Z</sub>

J'ai la réponse complète sur le volumétrique.

## Volumétrique avec le FEM SimNIBS : possible, mais hors du chemin publiable

Le solveur `TDCSLEADFIELD` **peut** produire un leadfield volumétrique — il suffit de mettre `lf.tissues = [2]` (volume de matière grise) au lieu de `lf.interpolation = "middle gm"` (qui projette sur la surface corticale). Donc techniquement, oui.

**Mais** le pont MNE officiel de SimNIBS (`setup_source_space` / `make_forward`) est **câblé en dur sur les surfaces corticales « central »**. Faire du volumétrique voudrait dire contourner ce pont et assembler nous-mêmes l'objet `Forward` à partir du leadfield brut — c'est-à-dire réintroduire exactement le « code maison à justifier dans un article » qu'on cherchait à éviter en choisissant le FEM.

**Conclusion :** pour rester sur la méthode établie et citable, on part sur le **cortical surfacique** (ce que tu as validé). Le volumétrique reste une extension possible plus tard, mais semi-custom. Je ne le code pas.

Je passe à l'implémentation. Je démarre l'installation de `mne` dans `simnibs_env` (nécessaire pour `make_forward`) en arrière-plan, et je vérifie en parallèle le format du montage d'électrodes.

### 📤 (tool results)  <sub>2026-07-20T22:08:03.788Z</sub>

<details><summary>📤 Tool result</summary>

```text
=== make_forward (utils_mne) : comment électrodes/ch_names/trans sont reliés:
def make_forward(
    forward: dict,
    src: Union[Path, str, mne.SourceSpaces],
    info: Union[Path, str, mne.Info],
    trans: Union[Path, str, mne.Transform],
):
    """Create an MNE forward object from a dictionary with forward information.
    Please note that the forward solution is converted to head coordinate
    system (using `trans`).

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    forward : dict
        Dictionary with forward solution information (as returned by
        `prepare_leadfield_for_eeg`).
    src : Path | str | mne.SourceSpaces
        Path to source space or SourceSpace object.
    info : Path | str | mne.Info
        Path to epochs, evoked, info, or raw file containing the correct info
        or an Info object.
    trans : Path | str | mne.Transform
        Path to transform or Transform object between head and MRI coordinte
        systems.

    RETURNS
    -------
    fwd : mne.Forward
        The forward object.

    NOTES
    -----
    Bad channels should *not* be included in the leadfield simulations. Should
    any such channels (as marked in the Info structure) be found in the forward
    solution, a warning will be issued.
    """
    if not isinstance(src, mne.SourceSpaces):
        src = mne.read_source_spaces(src)
    kwargs = {
        "bem": None,
        "bem_extra": "",
        "mindist": 0,
        "n_jobs": 1,
        "meg": False,
        "eeg": True,
        "ignore_ref": True,
        "allow_bem_none": True,
        "src": src,
    }
    if not isinstance(info, mne.Info):
        kwargs["info_extra"] = str(info)
        info = mne.io.read_info(info)
    else:
        kwargs["info_extra"] = "instance of Info"
    kwargs["info"] = info
    if not isinstance(trans, mne.Transform):
        kwargs["trans"] = str(trans)
        trans = mne.read_trans(trans)
        trans = mne.transforms._ensure_trans(trans, "mri", "head")
    else:
        kwargs["trans"] = "instance of Transform"
    kwargs["mri_head_t"] = trans

    # Check the inputs
    assert all(
        s["coord_frame"] == FIFF.FIFFV_COORD_MRI for s in src
    ), f"Source space should be in {FIFF.FIFFV_COORD_MRI} coordinate system"

    # Pick the EEG channels and check
    # picks = [info["ch_names"][i] for i in mne.pick_types(info, eeg=True)]
    # info.pick_channels(picks)
    assert (
        info["ch_names"] == forward["ch_names"]
    ), f"Inconsistencies between channels in Info and leadfield {info['ch_names']} and {forward['ch_names']}"
    bads_in_forward = tuple(filter(lambda x: x in info["bads"], forward["ch_names"]))
    if any(bads_in_forward):
        logger.warning(
            "Bad channels from Info found in forward solution: " f"{bads_in_forward}"
        )

    # fmt: off
    sensors, _, _, update_kwargs, _ = mne.forward._make_forward._prepare_for_forward(**kwargs)
    # fmt: on
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:11:21.451Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
The EEG cap positions and mesh scalp are actually in the same coordinate frame—the x and y ranges align closely, and the z discrepancy I noted earlier was just because the skin mesh extends down to the neck while the EEG positions only cover the upper head. So the EEG cap is definitely in mesh world coordinates, which confirms what I suspected. Charm also generated some fiducials data that I should look at.

Now I need to figure out how to map our digitized electrodes from head space into the mesh world frame. The path is: electrode in head space → apply MNE's transform to get to tkrRAS → then convert from tkrRAS to scanner RAS (which is the mesh world). The conversion between those two RAS spaces depends on the voxel-to-RAS matrices from the T1 image that MNE uses, but I need to be careful about which T1 defines the tkrRAS for the transform.

Actually, this reveals something critical: when SimNIBS runs make_forward, it loads the charm central GM surfaces (which are in mesh world / scanner RAS) and tells MNE these are in the "mri" frame. So SimNIBS is treating mesh world as MNE's "MRI" coordinate system — not FreeSurfer's tkrRAS. That means the trans I need should map directly from head to mesh world, and I don't need the intermediate tkrRAS conversion at all.

Since my current coregistration produces head → tkrRAS, I need to rework it to produce head → mesh world instead. That means running ICP against the SimNIBS scalp surface (already in mesh world) and using the fiducials from Fiducials.csv (which are already in mesh world), so the resulting transformation goes straight to where SimNIBS expects it.

This actually simplifies everything—everything lives in one consistent frame (mesh world, which SimNIBS calls "mri"). The scalp surface, fiducials, coregistered electrodes, and the forward model all work in the same coordinate system, so I just feed the trans (head → mesh world) directly to make_forward without any additional warping.

I can drop the tkrRAS and T1.mgz machinery entirely for the forward computation itself, though keeping T1.mgz around might still be useful for QC visualization since MNE's tools expect it in a standard subjects_dir layout.

The key insight is to set the coordinate frame consistently to "world" (mesh world / subject scanner RAS) throughout, which is what SimNIBS uses natively, and let the trans matrix handle the transformation—this bypasses FreeSurfer conventions entirely for the actual computation.

For the SPIKE, I can de-risk the FEM API by using charm's own subject-space electrode montage directly as real subject-space electrodes, sidestepping the full coreg rework and building the info object from those electrode names. Setting up the concrete spike plan: use the 10-10 cap with around 70 electrodes, run the FEM solve with modest subsampling in the background to keep it tractable, then construct the MNE info object with those cap positions in head frame and build the forward model from there.

The FEM solve itself is the bottleneck—reciprocity means one solve per electrode across an 800k-node mesh, which could take 30 minutes to 2 hours. I'm torn between validating with a smaller subset to move faster or using a moderate montage with more CPUs to keep it realistic. Let me just start the spike with the full pipeline and see how it goes.

Actually, I'm realizing the real risk isn't the FEM solve—SimNIBS's compute_tdcs_leadfield is solid. The risky part is make_forward, the coordinate handling, and how our electrode plumbing integrates with MNE. I need at least one leadfield to test make_forward, so let me kick off a smaller run.

I'll use a minimal montage with just the 10-20 system electrodes and coarse subsampling to generate a leadfield quickly, then validate that make_forward produces a valid forward model. Once that works, I can scale up. Let me write the spike script to run both the leadfield generation and make_forward in the background while I build out the module scaffolding.
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:11:38.599Z</sub>

<details><summary>🔧 Tool call: <code>Write</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\claude\\C--Users-USER-Documents-Henri-Mondor-IRM-to-MNE-python\\6c11610c-cac9-4396-8398-266fd741c3e0\\scratchpad\\spike_fem.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Spike: run a minimal FEM EEG leadfield on real charm output and convert to\nan MNE forward. De-risks the SimNIBS eeg.forward -> mne.Forward path.\n\nRuns in simnibs_env (has simnibs + mne). Uses charm's own subject-space 10-20\ncap as the montage, and an identity head<->mri trans (montage positions treated\nas head coords), so no coregistration is needed to validate the API.\n\"\"\"\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\nimport numpy as np\nimport mne\nimport simnibs\nfrom simnibs.eeg import forward as sim_fwd\nfrom simnibs.utils.csv_reader import _get_eeg_positions\n\nmne.set_log_level(\"ERROR\")\nM2M = Path(sys.argv[1])\nFEM = Path(sys.argv[2])\nFEM.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\n# Montage: charm's subject-space 10-20 cap (fewest electrodes -> fast spike).\ncap = M2M / \"eeg_positions\" / \"EEG10-20_Okamoto_2004.csv\"\npos = _get_eeg_positions(str(cap))  # {name: xyz} in mesh world (mm)\nnames = list(pos.keys())\nprint(f\"montage: {len(names)} electrodes -> {names}\")\n\nprint(\"=== compute_tdcs_leadfield (FEM, this is the slow part) ===\")\nleadfield = sim_fwd.compute_tdcs_leadfield(\n    str(M2M), str(FEM), str(cap),\n    subsampling=5000,             # coarse cortical source space for speed\n    point_electrodes=True,\n    run_kwargs=dict(save_mat=False, cpus=4),\n)\nprint(\"leadfield ->\", leadfield)\n\nprint(\"=== make_forward -> mne.Forward ===\")\n# info: EEG channels named as the montage, positions in 'head' (= mesh world here)\nmontage = mne.channels.make_dig_montage(\n    ch_pos={n: np.asarray(p) / 1000.0 for n, p in pos.items()},  # m\n    coord_frame=\"head\",\n)\ninfo = mne.create_info(names, 1000.0, \"eeg\")\ninfo.set_montage(montage)\ntrans = mne.transforms.Transform(\"head\", \"mri\")  # identity for the spike\n\nsrc, fwd, morph = sim_fwd.make_forward(\n    str(M2M), leadfield, out_format=\"mne\",\n    info=info, trans=trans, morph_to_fsaverage=None, write=False,\n)\nmne.write_forward_solution(str(FEM / \"spike-fwd.fif\"), fwd, overwrite=True)\n\ng = fwd[\"sol\"][\"data\"]\nprint(\"================= SPIKE RESULT =================\")\nprint(\"forward: %d sources x %d channels\" % (fwd[\"nsource\"], fwd[\"nchan\"]))\nprint(\"source space type:\", src[0][\"type\"], \"| n src spaces:\", len(src))\nprint(\"gain finite:\", bool(np.all(np.isfinite(g))), \"| max|g|=%.3e\" % np.abs(g).max())\nprint(\"wrote\", FEM / \"spike-fwd.fif\")\nprint(\"SPIKE OK\" if np.all(np.isfinite(g)) and fwd[\"nsource\"] > 100 else \"SPIKE PROBLEM\")\n"
}
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:18:58.101Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
The skin surface alone isn't a clean manifold—it has significant topological issues because it's an interface between the skin volume and surrounding tissues, creating non-manifold edges where it meets other structures like the eyes and neck boundary. Extracting just those tagged triangles gives a fragmented mess, so the better approach is to compute the boundary of the union of all tetrahedra instead.

Taking the outer boundary of all head volume tets (tags 1–10) gives me a clean, closed manifold that represents the true scalp surface—which is what my proto_mesh_bem.py script generates as "outer_skin" and should be topologically sound. For ICP coregistration, I technically only need scalp points to fit against digitized electrode positions, so even a slightly non-manifold surface would work, but MNE's head.fif expects a proper surface anyway. For the FEM pipeline, I should use the boundary-of-all-tets approach since it's clean and gives me the scalp representation I need for coregistration.

I'm realizing I should verify that the outer_skin boundary from proto_mesh_bem is actually clean before committing to it—I didn't get to run it before being interrupted. Let me check the spike output to see if it's progressed, then validate that the outer boundary is topologically sound.
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:20:55.067Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
The boundary mesh of the head has an Euler characteristic of -108, indicating roughly 55 handles—far from a simple sphere. This high genus comes from the SimNIBS mesh structure: the neck cut, internal cavities, and air pockets around the eyes all create topological handles. There are only 6 non-manifold edges, which is relatively minor, but the Euler value confirms this is a geometrically complex surface.

For ICP coregistration, though, I don't actually need a perfect closed manifold. The digitized scalp points are on the outer surface, and ICP just finds the closest points—internal cavity walls don't interfere with that fitting. Even the raw skin surface works fine for alignment purposes. The non-manifoldness is a mesh-topology detail that doesn't affect the coregistration task itself.

Actually, I'm realizing I might be overcomplicating this. Instead of relying on MNE's coregistration GUI, I could use the fiducial landmarks directly—SimNIBS already gives me the subject-space fiducials (Nz, LPA, RPA) in the Fiducials.csv file. I can align the digitized fiducials to those subject fiducials to get the transformation without needing ICP at all.

But ICP on the skin surface (tag 1005) would refine that initial alignment, and the non-manifoldness doesn't matter for the algorithm. So I'll stick with using the skin surface points for ICP and move forward instead of getting stuck on topology details.

The FEM solve is still running—SimNIBS is probably buffering output or logging elsewhere, so I'll wait for the spike output to appear.

While that processes, I could start building the forward model module to match the spike's API structure, but I'm hesitant to write too much code before confirming the spike works. Instead, let me verify that MNE's coreg actually handles the skin surface well for ICP, which is something I can test independently. I can validate the coregistration approach by testing the head-to-mesh transformation using the sample's digitized electrodes and fiducials against charm's fiducials, then refining with ICP on the skin surface — this is independent of the spike and worth doing quickly before things get too late in the sequence.

But stepping back, I've done extensive investigation and the spike is running, so the clearest path is to wait for it to confirm the FEM-to-MNE forward works on real data, then productionize into modules, handle coregistration in mesh-world coordinates, and tackle rewiring, inverse adaptation, tests, and docs. This is a multi-hour effort with a long FEM solve in the middle, so I should report status to the user and continue once the spike completes.

Before I do that, let me verify the spike didn't silently fail by checking if the process is still alive and whether SimNIBS is actively writing logs to the FEM directory.

But given past surprises with real data—qform issues, 4D formats, surface handling—I should wait for the spike to fully complete and confirm make_forward produces a valid forward solution end-to-end before finalizing the module. While waiting for the notification, I'll prepare the coregistration validation independently—validating the skin surface and CHARM fiducials transformation from head space to mesh world coordinates, which is needed regardless and doesn't depend on the spike finishing.

For the coregistration itself, I'm weighing two approaches: either leverage MNE's Coregistration tool by setting up a subjects_dir where the "mri" frame is already mesh world, or implement a direct rigid alignment using Kabsch/Procrustes to map the digitized fiducials to CHARM's fiducials, then refine with ICP to match digitized head points to the skin surface. The second approach is cleaner and avoids fighting FreeSurfer conventions, so I'll implement a simple rigid coregistration pipeline with fiducial alignment followed by iterative ICP refinement. So I can actually reuse my existing coregistration pipeline almost entirely—I just need to feed MNE's Coregistration the head surface and fiducials both expressed in mesh-world coordinates and labeled as the "mri" frame, which makes the resulting transformation exactly what SimNIBS expects for the forward model. The coregistration fitting logic stays the same, so this is actually a minimal refactor. My plan is to build out the forward model code once the spike pattern is ready, then create a small helper to extract the skin surface and fiducials from the mesh in mesh-world coordinates, rewire coregistration to use those sources, adapt the inverse solver for surface estimates, and finally update the pipeline to retire the old surfaces and BEM approach. First I want to validate that this mesh-world coregistration strategy actually works on real data with the digitized electrodes and charm fiducials.

For that validation I need the sample montage in head coordinates, the charm fiducials in mesh coordinates, and the skin surface as the head reference. Running MNE's coregistration with these should give me a transformation from head to mesh-world, and I can check if the residuals look reasonable. The question is whether MNE's coregistration requires the full subject directory structure with T1.mgz or if it can work with just the fiducials and head surface.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:24:22.956Z</sub>

<details><summary>🔧 Tool call: <code>Write</code></summary>

```json
{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\_simnibs_fem_helper.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Standalone helper that runs inside SimNIBS' own Python (simnibs_env).\n\nThe pipeline (in the irm2mne env) invokes this via subprocess because it needs\nboth `simnibs` and `mne`, which live together only in the SimNIBS environment.\nIt must therefore import nothing from the rest of mri2mne -- only simnibs, mne\nand numpy.\n\nContract (all paths passed as JSON on argv[1]):\n    {\n      \"m2m_dir\": ...,          # charm output\n      \"fem_dir\": ...,          # clean output dir for the leadfield\n      \"montage_csv\": ...,      # electrodes in mesh world, SimNIBS cap format\n      \"info_fif\": ...,         # mne Info/raw/evoked with the EEG channels\n      \"trans_fif\": ...,        # head<->mri (mesh world) transform\n      \"out_fwd\": ...,          # where to write -fwd.fif\n      \"out_src\": ...,          # where to write -src.fif\n      \"subsampling\": 10000,    # cortical source points per hemisphere (or null)\n      \"cpus\": 4,\n      \"morph_to_fsaverage\": 5  # or null\n    }\n\nPrints a JSON result dict on the last stdout line.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport json\nimport shutil\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\n\ndef main() -> int:\n    cfg = json.loads(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n\n    import mne\n    import numpy as np\n    from simnibs.eeg import forward as sim_fwd\n\n    mne.set_log_level(\"ERROR\")\n\n    fem_dir = Path(cfg[\"fem_dir\"])\n    # SimNIBS refuses to overwrite an existing leadfield group in the hdf5, so\n    # the FEM directory must be clean before every run.\n    if fem_dir.exists():\n        shutil.rmtree(fem_dir)\n    fem_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\n    # --- FEM leadfield (the slow part) -------------------------------------\n    leadfield = sim_fwd.compute_tdcs_leadfield(\n        cfg[\"m2m_dir\"],\n        str(fem_dir),\n        cfg[\"montage_csv\"],\n        subsampling=cfg.get(\"subsampling\"),\n        point_electrodes=True,\n        run_kwargs=dict(save_mat=False, cpus=int(cfg.get(\"cpus\", 1))),\n    )\n\n    # --- Convert to an MNE forward -----------------------------------------\n    info = mne.io.read_info(cfg[\"info_fif\"])\n    trans = mne.read_trans(cfg[\"trans_fif\"])\n\n    src, fwd, morph = sim_fwd.make_forward(\n        cfg[\"m2m_dir\"],\n        leadfield,\n        out_format=\"mne\",\n        info=info,\n        trans=trans,\n        morph_to_fsaverage=cfg.get(\"morph_to_fsaverage\"),\n        write=False,\n    )\n\n    mne.write_forward_solution(cfg[\"out_fwd\"], fwd, overwrite=True)\n    mne.write_source_spaces(cfg[\"out_src\"], src, overwrite=True)\n    if morph is not None and cfg.get(\"out_morph\"):\n        morph.save(cfg[\"out_morph\"], overwrite=True)\n\n    gain = fwd[\"sol\"][\"data\"]\n    result = {\n        \"ok\": bool(np.all(np.isfinite(gain))),\n        \"n_sources\": int(fwd[\"nsource\"]),\n        \"n_channels\": int(fwd[\"nchan\"]),\n        \"leadfield\": str(leadfield),\n        \"src_type\": src[0][\"type\"],\n        \"gain_max_abs\": float(np.abs(gain).max()),\n    }\n    print(\"MRI2MNE_RESULT \" + json.dumps(result))\n    return 0 if result[\"ok\"] else 1\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    raise SystemExit(main())\n"
}
```
</details>

Something's triggering a second invocation of the script. The real issue is that `compute_tdcs_leadfield` with `cpus=4` triggers multiprocessing, and on Windows this re-imports the entire spike_fem.py module in worker processes — since my script runs everything at the top level without a `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard, each worker re-executes the whole thing, causing a second leadfield creation and the "group already exists" conflict. My helper script has the guard, so I need to add it to spike_fem.py as well.

Now I'm going to test the helper directly (which already has the guard) by creating the necessary input files — a montage CSV from the 10-20 cap, an info FIF built from that cap, and an identity transform — then running the helper in the SimNIBS environment.

I'll start with cpus=1 to avoid multiprocessing complications and confirm the helper works reliably before testing with higher CPU counts.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:25:24.240Z</sub>

Vrai bug trouvé, et il **valide la conception du helper** : mon script de spike n'avait pas de garde `if __name__ == "__main__"`. Avec `cpus=4`, SimNIBS utilise le multiprocessing, qui sous Windows **réimporte le module principal** → le script entier se relançait dans chaque worker → deux leadfields concurrents → conflit hdf5. 

Mon helper de production `_simnibs_fem_helper.py` a **déjà la garde**. Je teste donc directement le helper (ce que la prod utilisera), avec `cpus=1` d'abord pour isoler. Je nettoie les processus restants et je prépare les entrées.

---

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:26:35.110Z</sub>

Le helper de production tourne (cpus=1 pour éviter le multiprocessing le temps de valider). Je serai notifié à la fin. Point d'étape sur la bascule FEM, car j'ai bien avancé :

## Où en est la refonte FEM

**Validé / verrouillé :**
- L'API officielle SimNIBS `compute_tdcs_leadfield` → `make_forward` → `mne.Forward` existe et le **solveur FEM tourne sur la vraie sortie `charm`** (matrice assemblée, leadfield en cours).
- **Repère de coordonnées** : le monde-maillage SimNIBS = le repère « MRI » de MNE. Conséquence majeure : je **réutilise ma coregistration existante** presque telle quelle (surface de scalp + fiduciaux en coords maillage).
- `charm` fournit déjà les **fiduciaux sujet** (`Fiducials.csv`) → plus besoin de mon warp MNI.
- Pour la coreg, la surface de peau brute suffit (points pour l'ICP), pas de casse-tête topologique.

**Deux vrais bugs attrapés en route :**
- Dossier FEM non-propre → conflit hdf5 (le helper nettoie désormais avant chaque run).
- **Multiprocessing Windows** : sans garde `if __name__=="__main__"`, SimNIBS réimportait et relançait tout le script dans chaque worker. Le helper de production a la garde — problème réglé par conception.

**Écrit :** `_simnibs_fem_helper.py` — le script autonome qui tourne dans `simnibs_env` et produit le `-fwd.fif` que le pipeline (irm2mne) consomme ensuite.

**Il reste, une fois le helper confirmé :** le module `simnibs_forward.py` (côté irm2mne, écrit le montage + appelle le helper), la bascule coreg (petite), l'adaptation de `inverse.py` au surfacique, le recâblage pipeline/wrapper, et le retrait de `surfaces.py`/BEM.

Je continue dès que le helper me confirme un `-fwd.fif` valide sur données réelles. À suivre 🤝

---

  "content": "\"\"\"FEM EEG forward via SimNIBS, driven from the pipeline environment.\n\nThis is the pipeline-side (irm2mne) half. It prepares the inputs, then hands\noff to `_simnibs_fem_helper.py`, which runs inside SimNIBS' own Python because\nthe leadfield solver and its MNE bridge live only there. The helper writes a\n`-fwd.fif`; we read it back here.\n\nCoordinate frames: SimNIBS treats the mesh world (subject scanner RAS) as MNE's\n\"MRI\" frame. The coregistration therefore produces a head->mesh-world trans,\nand the leadfield electrodes are the digitised electrodes mapped into mesh world\nwith that trans. `make_forward` (in the helper) uses the same trans + info to\nexpress the forward in head coordinates.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport json\nimport logging\nimport subprocess\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\n\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\n\n\nclass ForwardError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when the FEM forward cannot be produced.\"\"\"\n\n\ndef simnibs_python(bin_dir: Path | None) -> Path:\n    \"\"\"Locate the Python interpreter of the SimNIBS environment.\n\n    The leadfield + MNE bridge must run there. `bin_dir` is the SimNIBS Scripts\n    folder (holds charm); its interpreter sits one level up.\n    \"\"\"\n    if bin_dir is None:\n        raise ForwardError(\n            \"simnibs.bin_dir must be set for the FEM forward: it locates the \"\n            \"SimNIBS Python that runs the leadfield solver.\"\n        )\n    candidate = bin_dir.parent / \"python.exe\"\n    if not candidate.is_file():\n        candidate = bin_dir.parent / \"bin\" / \"python\"  # non-Windows layout\n    if not candidate.is_file():\n        raise ForwardError(\n            f\"Could not find the SimNIBS Python next to {bin_dir} (looked for \"\n            f\"{candidate}). Point simnibs.bin_dir at the env's Scripts folder.\"\n        )\n    return candidate\n\n\ndef write_montage_csv(\n    info, trans, montage_csv: Path, logger: logging.Logger\n) -> list[str]:\n    \"\"\"Write electrodes to a SimNIBS cap CSV in mesh-world (subject) coords.\n\n    The electrode positions in `info` are head-frame metres; `trans` maps head\n    to MRI (= mesh world). We convert to mesh-world millimetres, which is what\n    the leadfield solver expects, and keep the channel order so it matches the\n    forward's channel order later.\n    \"\"\"\n    import csv\n\n    import mne\n    from mne.transforms import _ensure_trans, apply_trans\n\n    head_mri_t = _ensure_trans(trans, \"head\", \"mri\")\n\n    picks = mne.pick_types(info, eeg=True, exclude=[])\n    names = [info[\"ch_names\"][i] for i in picks]\n    if not names:\n        raise ForwardError(\"Info contains no EEG channels for the montage.\")\n\n    montage_csv.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    with open(montage_csv, \"w\", newline=\"\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as fh:\n        writer = csv.writer(fh)\n        for i in picks:\n            loc_head_m = np.asarray(info[\"chs\"][i][\"loc\"][:3], dtype=float)\n            if not np.all(np.isfinite(loc_head_m)) or np.allclose(loc_head_m, 0):\n                raise ForwardError(\n                    f\"Channel {info['ch_names'][i]} has no position; the \"\n                    \"digitisation did not cover every EEG channel.\"\n                )\n            pos_mesh_mm = apply_trans(head_mri_t, loc_head_m) * 1000.0\n            writer.writerow([\"Electrode\", *pos_mesh_mm, info[\"ch_names\"][i]])\n\n    logger.info(\"Wrote SimNIBS montage with %d electrodes -> %s\",\n                len(names), montage_csv.name)\n    return names\n\n\ndef make_fem_forward(\n    paths: SubjectPaths,\n    info,\n    trans,\n    bin_dir: Path | None,\n    logger: logging.Logger,\n    subsampling: int | None = 10000,\n    cpus: int = 1,\n    morph_to_fsaverage: int | None = 5,\n    timeout_s: int = 21600,\n) -> t

{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\_simnibs_mesh_helper.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Standalone helper (runs in simnibs_env) to extract coregistration inputs.\n\nPulls the scalp surface and the subject-space fiducials out of the SimNIBS head\nmodel so the pipeline can coregister the digitised electrodes without any BEM\nsurface building. Everything is in mesh-world coordinates (subject scanner RAS),\nwhich SimNIBS' MNE bridge treats as the \"MRI\" frame.\n\nImports only simnibs + numpy. Config via JSON on argv[1]:\n    {\"m2m_dir\": ..., \"out_scalp_npz\": ..., \"out_fiducials_json\": ...}\n\"\"\"\n\nimport glob\nimport json\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\n\n\ndef main() -> int:\n    cfg = json.loads(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    import simnibs\n    from simnibs.utils.csv_reader import _get_eeg_positions\n\n    m2m = Path(cfg[\"m2m_dir\"])\n    mesh_files = list(m2m.glob(\"*.msh\")) or list(m2m.glob(\"**/*.msh\"))\n    if not mesh_files:\n        raise SystemExit(f\"No .msh head model found in {m2m}\")\n    mesh = simnibs.read_msh(str(mesh_files[0]))\n\n    # Scalp surface: the skin interface triangles (tag 1005). Not necessarily a\n    # closed manifold, but ICP only needs surface points to fit against.\n    tri = (mesh.elm.elm_type == 2) & (mesh.elm.tag1 == 1005)\n    faces = mesh.elm.node_number_list[tri][:, :3] - 1\n    used = np.unique(faces)\n    remap = np.full(mesh.nodes.nr, -1, np.int64)\n    remap[used] = np.arange(len(used))\n    rr_mm = mesh.nodes.node_coord[used]          # mesh world, mm\n    tris = remap[faces]\n    np.savez(cfg[\"out_scalp_npz\"], rr_mm=rr_mm, tris=tris)\n\n    # Fiducials that charm warped into subject space.\n    fids = {}\n    fid_csv = glob.glob(str(m2m / \"eeg_positions\" / \"Fiducials.csv\"))\n    if fid_csv:\n        raw = _get_eeg_positions(fid_csv[0])  # {name: xyz mm}\n        alias = {\"nz\": \"nasion\", \"nasion\": \"nasion\", \"lpa\": \"lpa\", \"rpa\": \"rpa\"}\n        for name, xyz in raw.items():\n            key = alias.get(name.strip().lower())\n            if key:\n                fids[key] = [float(v) for v in xyz]\n    Path(cfg[\"out_fiducials_json\"]).write_text(json.dumps(fids), encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\n    print(\"MRI2MNE_RESULT \" + json.dumps({\n        \"n_scalp_verts\": int(len(rr_mm)),\n        \"n_scalp_tris\": int(len(tris)),\n        \"fiducials\": sorted(fids),\n    }))\n    return 0\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    raise SystemExit(main())\n"
}

Bon point d'étape — le plus dur est fait. Je fais le point honnêtement, car la suite est de la productionnisation solide mais volumineuse.

## ✅ La voie FEM est prouvée sur données réelles

Le point à risque est franchi. Sur la vraie sortie `charm` du sample, la chaîne complète tourne : **leadfield FEM → `make_forward` → `mne.Forward` de 10000 sources corticales × 19 canaux**, gain fini, relu sans erreur dans l'environnement du pipeline. La méthode officielle SimNIBS→MNE fonctionne — c'est exactement le socle citable que tu voulais.

## Trois vrais problèmes attrapés et réglés au passage

1. **Dossier FEM non-propre** → conflit hdf5. Le helper nettoie avant chaque run.
2. **Multiprocessing Windows** : sans garde `__main__`, SimNIBS relançait tout le script dans chaque worker. Le helper a la garde.
3. **Incompatibilité MNE 1.12** : SimNIBS appelle des API privées MNE qui ont bougé (lazy-loading + une fonction déplacée) et stocke `mri_head_t` à l'envers (head→MRI au lieu de MRI→head, que le writer MNE refuse). Réglé par un shim documenté (qui pointe vers les fonctions existantes, ne réimplémente rien) + correction d'orientation.

## Ce qui est construit

- **`_simnibs_fem_helper.py`** — le solveur FEM + pont MNE, tournant dans `simnibs_env`, produit le `-fwd.fif`. Validé.
- **`simnibs_forward.py`** — le pilote côté pipeline : montage électrodes→SimNIBS, appel sous-processus, relecture du forward.
- **`_simnibs_mesh_helper.py`** — extraction scalp + fiduciaux du maillage pour la coreg (`charm` fournit déjà les fiduciaux sujet).
- `mne` installé dans `simnibs_env`.

## Ce qui reste (productionnisation, ~lower-risk)

| Tâche | Statut |
|---|---|
| Coregistration en repère maillage (réutilise l'existant + scalp/fiduciaux SimNIBS) | à câbler |
| `inverse.py` : adapter au surfacique (loose=0.2, stc `-lh/-rh`, pic sur surface) | à faire |
| Recâbler `pipeline.py`/`wrapper.py` : `charm` complet → coreg → forward FEM → inverse | à faire |
| Retirer `surfaces.py` + BEM/volumétrique ; MAJ config, tests, README | à faire |
| Re-validation end-to-end complète (implique un nouveau run FEM lent) | à faire |

Il y a encore un vrai morceau de travail, et la re-validation finale nécessitera un nouveau calcul FEM (lent). Mais l'incertitude technique est levée : on **sait** que ça marche. 

Je continue sur le câblage de la coregistration et l'adaptation de l'inverse. Veux-tu que je pousse jusqu'au bout d'un trait, ou tu préfères valider ce point d'étape d'abord ?

### 📤 (tool results)  <sub>2026-07-20T22:52:34.203Z</sub>

<details><summary>📤 Tool result</summary>

```text
=== prepare_montage + mne_montage_to_simnibs_montage (utils_mne 488-620):
def prepare_montage(
    fname: Union[Path, str],
    info: Union[Path, str, mne.Info],
    trans: Union[Path, str, mne.Transform],
):
    """Prepare a montage file from an MNE-Python objects. The electrode
    positions in `info` are transformed from head to MRI coordinate system.

    PARAMETERS
    ----------
    fname : str |
        The name of the file to write. If it does not end with '.csv', this
        will be added.
    info : str | mne.Info
        Path to epochs, evoked, info, or raw file containing the correct info
        or an Info object.
    trans : str | mne.Transform
        Path to transform or Transform object between head and MRI coordinte
        systems.

    RETURNS
    -------
    None
    """
    fname = Path(fname)
    if fname.suffix != ".csv":
        fname = fname.with_suffix(".csv")
    if not isinstance(info, mne.Info):
        info = mne.io.read_info(info)
    if not isinstance(trans, mne.Transform):
        trans = mne.read_trans(trans)

    # Electrodes are in head coordinates and m so transform to MRI coordinates
    # and mm
    trans = mne.transforms._ensure_trans(trans, "head", "mri")
    electrodes = {
        ch["ch_name"]: 1e3 * mne.transforms.apply_trans(trans, ch["loc"][:3])
        for ch in info["chs"]
    }

    ch_types = ["Electrode"] * len(electrodes)
    ch_pos = np.array(list(electrodes.values()))
    ch_names = list(electrodes.keys())

    fid_pos = mne.viz._3d._fiducial_coords(info["dig"])
    if fid_pos.size > 0:
        fid_pos = 1e3 * mne.transforms.apply_trans(trans, fid_pos)
        mapper = {
            FIFF.FIFFV_POINT_LPA: "LPA",
            FIFF.FIFFV_POINT_NASION: "Nz",
            FIFF.FIFFV_POINT_RPA: "RPA",
            FIFF.FIFFV_POINT_INION: "Iz",
        }
        fid_names = [mapper[i] for i in mne.viz._3d.FIDUCIAL_ORDER]

        ch_types += ["Fiducial"] * fid_pos.size
        ch_pos = np.concatenate((ch_pos, fid_pos), axis=0)
        ch_names += fid_names

    csv_reader.write_csv_positions(
        fname,
        ch_types,
        ch_pos,
        ch_names,
    )


def simnibs_montage_to_mne_montage(montage, coord_frame="unknown"):
    valid_entries = {"Electrode", "ReferenceElectrode"}
    if len(montage.ch_names) > 0 and len(montage.ch_pos) > 0:
        ch_pos = {
            n: p
            for n, p, t in zip(
                montage.ch_names, 1e-3 * montage.ch_pos, montage.ch_types
            )
            if t in valid_entries
        }
    else:
        ch_pos = None

    landmarks = {k: None for k in MNE_LANDMARKS}
    if montage.landmarks:
        for k in landmarks:
            landmarks[k] = (
                1e-3 * montage.landmarks[landmarks_mapper["mne_to_simnibs"][k]]
            )

    return mne.channels.make_dig_montage(ch_pos, coord_frame=coord_frame, **landmarks)


def mne_montage_to_simnibs_montage(montage, name=None):
    d = montage.get_positions()
    ch_names = list(d["ch_pos"].keys())
    ch_pos = [p * 1e3 for p in d["ch_pos"].values()]
    ch_types = ["Electrode"] * len(ch_names)
    try:
        landmarks = {
            landmarks_mapper["mne_to_simnibs"][k]: 1e3 * d[k] for k in MNE_LANDMARKS
        }
    except TypeError:
        # at least one of nasion, lpa, or rpa is None
        landmarks = None
    return Montage(name, ch_names, ch_pos, ch_types, landmarks)
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-20T22:53:00.545Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
I found exactly what I need — SimNIBS has a public `prepare_montage` function that takes MNE info and a head-to-MRI transformation, converts electrode coordinates to the mesh space in millimeters, and writes out the montage CSV with fiducials. This is precisely what my custom `write_montage_csv` function was doing.

I should replace my custom implementation with a direct call to SimNIBS's `prepare_montage` from `simnibs.eeg.utils_mne`. The helper script can call this function to prepare the montage, then pass it to `compute_tdcs_leadfield` and the forward model computation — keeping all the SimNIBS-specific work consolidated in one place using their own functions. This way the pipeline-side code just handles writing the info and transformation files, then delegates everything else to the helper.

I also need to refactor the mesh helper to use SimNIBS's public API: `mesh.crop_mesh(tags=[1005])` for extracting the scalp instead of raw numpy operations, and `read_csv_positions` for reading fiducials instead of the private `_get_eeg_positions` function. ner. I need to check the `read_csv_positions` signature for fiducials, then refactor accordingly. Actually, `prepare_montage` writes fiducials into the montage CSV, but for the leadfield computation I only want electrodes since fiducials are landmarks and would create spurious leadfield columns. The leadfield computation filters for "Electrode" type entries, so including fiducials in the montage shouldn't cause issues downstream.

Now I'm realizing the key consistency check: `prepare_montage` outputs all channels from info (electrodes) plus fiducials from info['dig'], the leadfield computes only for electrodes, and `make_forward` matches info ch_names to forward ch_names. As long as info ch_names contains only the electrodes and not fiducials, everything stays consistent. Since `prepare_montage` writes electrodes using info ch_names, this should work fine. Let me refactor the FEM helper to use `prepare_montage`.

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\simnibs_forward.py",
  "old_string": "def write_montage_csv(\n    info, trans, montage_csv: Path, logger: logging.Logger\n) -> list[str]:\n    \"\"\"Write electrodes to a SimNIBS cap CSV in mesh-world (subject) coords.\n\n    The electrode positions in `info` are head-frame metres; `trans` maps head\n    to MRI (= mesh world). We convert to mesh-world millimetres, which is what\n    the leadfield solver expects, and keep the channel order so it matches the\n    forward's channel order later.\n    \"\"\"\n    import csv\n\n    import mne\n    from mne.transforms import _ensure_trans, apply_trans\n\n    head_mri_t = _ensure_trans(trans, \"head\", \"mri\")\n\n    picks = mne.pick_types(info, eeg=True, exclude=[])\n    names = [info[\"ch_names\"][i] for i in picks]\n    if not names:\n        raise ForwardError(\"Info contains no EEG channels for the montage.\")\n\n    montage_csv.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    with open(montage_csv, \"w\", newline=\"\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as fh:\n        writer = csv.writer(fh)\n        for i in picks:\n            loc_head_m = np.asarray(info[\"chs\"][i][\"loc\"][:3], dtype=float)\n            if not np.all(np.isfinite(loc_head_m)) or np.allclose(loc_head_m, 0):\n                raise ForwardError(\n                    f\"Channel {info['ch_names'][i]} has no position; the \"\n                    \"digitisation did not cover every EEG channel.\"\n                )\n            pos_mesh_mm = apply_trans(head_mri_t, loc_head_m) * 1000.0\n            writer.writerow([\"Electrode\", *pos_mesh_mm, info[\"ch_names\"][i]])\n\n    logger.info(\"Wrote SimNIBS montage with %d electrodes -> %s\",\n                len(names), montage_csv.name)\n    return names\n\n\ndef make_fem_forward(",
  "new_string": "def make_fem_forward("
}
```

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\_simnibs_mesh_helper.py",
  "old_string": "def main() -> int:\n    cfg = json.loads(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    import simnibs\n    from simnibs.utils.csv_reader import _get_eeg_positions\n\n    m2m = Path(cfg[\"m2m_dir\"])\n    mesh_files = list(m2m.glob(\"*.msh\")) or list(m2m.glob(\"**/*.msh\"))\n    if not mesh_files:\n        raise SystemExit(f\"No .msh head model found in {m2m}\")\n    mesh = simnibs.read_msh(str(mesh_files[0]))\n\n    # Scalp surface: the skin interface triangles (tag 1005). Not necessarily a\n    # closed manifold, but ICP only needs surface points to fit against.\n    tri = (mesh.elm.elm_type == 2) & (mesh.elm.tag1 == 1005)\n    faces = mesh.elm.node_number_list[tri][:, :3] - 1\n    used = np.unique(faces)\n    remap = np.full(mesh.nodes.nr, -1, np.int64)\n    remap[used] = np.arange(len(used))\n    rr_mm = mesh.nodes.node_coord[used]          # mesh world, mm\n    tris = remap[faces]\n    np.savez(cfg[\"out_scalp_npz\"], rr_mm=rr_mm, tris=tris)\n\n    # Fiducials that charm warped into subject space.\n    fids = {}\n    fid_csv = glob.glob(str(m2m / \"eeg_positions\" / \"Fiducials.csv\"))\n    if fid_csv:\n        raw = _get_eeg_positions(fid_csv[0])  # {name: xyz mm}\n        alias = {\"nz\": \"nasion\", \"nasion\": \"nasion\", \"lpa\": \"lpa\", \"rpa\": \"rpa\"}\n        for name, xyz in raw.items():\n            key = alias.get(name.strip().lower())\n            if key:\n                fids[key] = [float(v) for v in xyz]\n    Path(cfg[\"out_fiducials_json\"]).write_text(json.dumps(fids), encoding=\"utf-8\")",
  "new_string": "def main() -> int:\n    cfg = json.loads(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    import simnibs\n    from simnibs.utils.csv_reader import read_csv_positions\n\n    m2m = Path(cfg[\"m2m_dir\"])\n    mesh_files = list(m2m.glob(\"*.msh\")) or list(m2m.glob(\"**/*.msh\"))\n    if not mesh_files:\n        raise SystemExit(f\"No .msh head model found in {m2m}\")\n    mesh = simnibs.read_msh(str(mesh_files[0]))\n\n    # Scalp surface via SimNIBS' own crop_mesh (skin surface tag 1005). Not\n    # necessarily a closed manifold, but ICP only needs surface points to fit\n    # against. Triangle node indices are 1-based in the mesh; make them 0-based\n    # and compact for a standalone surface.\n    skin = mesh.crop_mesh(tags=[1005])\n    tri = skin.elm.elm_type == 2\n    faces = skin.elm.node_number_list[tri][:, :3] - 1\n    used = np.unique(faces)\n    remap = np.full(skin.nodes.nr, -1, np.int64)\n    remap[used] = np.arange(len(used))\n    rr_mm = skin.nodes.node_coord[used]          # mesh world, mm\n    tris = remap[faces]\n    np.savez(cfg[\"out_scalp_npz\"], rr_mm=rr_mm, tris=tris)\n\n    # Fiducials that charm warped into subject space, read with SimNIBS' public\n    # csv reader. read_csv_positions returns (types, coords, extra, names).\n    fids = {}\n    fid_csv = glob.glob(str(m2m / \"eeg_positions\" / \"Fiducials.csv\"))\n    if fid_csv:\n        _types, coords, _extra, names = read_csv_positions(fid_csv[0])\n        alias = {\"nz\": \"nasion\", \"nasion\": \"nasion\", \"lpa\": \"lpa\", \"rpa\": \"rpa\"}\n        for name, xyz in zip(names, np.asarray(coords, dtype=float)):\n            key = alias.get(str(name).strip().lower())\n            if key:\n                fids[key] = [float(v) for v in xyz[:3]]\n    Path(cfg[\"out_fiducials_json\"]).write_text(json.dumps(fids), encoding=\"utf-8\")"
}
```

Everything is\nin mesh-world coordinates, which SimNIBS treats as MNE's \"MRI\" frame.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport json\nimport logging\nimport subprocess\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\n\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\nfrom .simnibs_forward import ForwardError, simnibs_python\n\n\ndef extract_coreg_inputs(\n    paths: SubjectPaths,\n    bin_dir: Path | None,\n    logger: logging.Logger,\n    timeout_s: int = 1800,\n) -> dict[str, list[float]]:\n    \"\"\"Write bem/<subject>-head.fif and -fiducials.fif from the SimNIBS mesh.\n\n    Returns the fiducial dictionary (mesh-world mm). The head surface is the\n    SimNIBS skin surface; the fiducials are the ones charm warped into subject\n    space. Reuses MNE's coregistration downstream, unchanged.\n    \"\"\"\n    import mne\n    from mne.io.constants import FIFF\n    from mne.surface import complete_surface_info\n\n    py = simnibs_python(bin_dir)\n    scalp_npz = paths.root / \"coreg\" / \"scalp.npz\"\n    fids_json = paths.root / \"coreg\" / \"fiducials.json\"\n    scalp_npz.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\n    cfg = {\n        \"m2m_dir\": str(paths.m2m_dir),\n        \"out_scalp_npz\": str(scalp_npz),\n        \"out_fiducials_json\": str(fids_json),\n    }\n    cfg_path = paths.root / \"coreg\" / \"mesh_config.json\"\n    cfg_path.write_text(json.dumps(cfg), encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\n    helper = Path(__file__).with_name(\"_simnibs_mesh_helper.py\")\n    proc = subprocess.run(\n        [str(py), str(helper), str(cfg_path)],\n        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=timeout_s, check=False,\n    )\n    if proc.returncode != 0 or not scalp_npz.is_file():\n        tail = \"\\n\".join((proc.stderr or \"\").splitlines()[-12:])\n        raise ForwardError(\n            f\"SimNIBS mesh extraction failed (exit {proc.returncode}):\\n{tail}\"\n        )\n\n    # 1	"""Inverse operator and source estimates -- the actual source analysis.
2	
3	Combines our forward model, the noise covariance and the EEG data into a
4	distributed inverse solution over the volume source space. Minimum-norm family
5	(MNE / dSPM / sLORETA / eLORETA); an LCMV beamformer would be the natural
6	alternative for volumetric localisation and is noted in the README.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import logging
12	from pathlib import Path
13	
14	import numpy as np
15	
16	from .paths import SubjectPaths
17	
18	_INVERSE_METHODS = ("MNE", "dSPM", "sLORETA", "eLORETA")
19	
20	
21	class InverseError(RuntimeError):
22	    """Raised when the inverse operator or source estimate cannot be built."""
23	
24	
25	def make_inverse(
26	    paths: SubjectPaths,
27	    info,
28	    noise_cov,
29	    logger: logging.Logger,
30	    loose: float = 1.0,
31	    depth: float = 0.8,
32	):
33	    """Build and save the inverse operator.
34	
35	    `loose=1.0` (free orientation) is the right default for a volume source
36	    space: unlike a cortical surface, a volume grid has no normal direction to
37	    constrain dipoles to.
38	    """
39	    import mne
40	    from mne.minimum_norm import make_inverse_operator, write_inverse_operator
41	
42	    fwd = mne.read_forward_solution(str(paths.forward), verbose="ERROR")
43	
44	    try:
45	        inv = make_inverse_operator(
46	            info, fwd, noise_cov, loose=loose, depth=depth, verbose="ERROR",
47	        )
48	    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
49	        raise InverseError(
50	            f"Could not build the inverse operator: {exc}. A common cause is a "
51	            "mismatch between the EEG channels and the forward model's channels."
52	        ) from exc
53	
54	    paths.inverse.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
55	    write_inverse_operator(str(paths.inverse), inv, verbose="ERROR")
56	    logger.info("Wrote inverse operator -> %s", paths.inverse)
57	    return inv
58	
59	
60	def apply_inverse_evoked(
61	    paths: SubjectPaths,
62	    evoked,
63	    inv,
64	    method: str,
65	    snr: float,
66	    logger: logging.Logger,
67	):
68	    """Apply the inverse to an Evoked, producing a volume source estimate."""
69	    import mne
70	    from mne.minimum_norm import apply_inverse
71	
72	    if method not in _INVERSE_METHODS:
73	        raise InverseError(
74	            f"Unknown inverse method '{method}'. Choose from {_INVERSE_METHODS}."
75	        )
76	
77	    lambda2 = 1.0 / snr ** 2
78	    stc = apply_inverse(evoked, inv, lambda2=lambda2, method=method,
79	                        verbose="ERROR")
80	
81	    paths.source_estimate.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
82	    stc.save(str(paths.source_estimate), overwrite=True, verbose="ERROR")
83	    logger.info("Wrote %s source estimate -> %s-vl.stc",
84	                method, paths.source_estimate)
85	
86	    if not np.all(np.isfinite(stc.data)):
87	        raise InverseError("Source estimate contains non-finite values.")
88	    return stc
89	
90	
91	def peak_location(stc, src, logger: logging.Logger) -> dict[str, float]:
92	    """Locate the strongest source, in scanner-RAS millimetres.

97	    vert_idx, t_peak = stc.get_peak(vert_as_index=True, time_as_index=False)
98

106	        t_peak, *peak_mri_mm, amplitude,
107	    )
108	    return {
109	        "peak_time_s": float(t_peak),

111	        "peak_mri_y_mm":

  "content": "\"\"\"Inverse operator and source estimates -- the actual source analysis.\n\nCombines the SimNIBS FEM forward, the noise covariance and the EEG data into a\ndistributed inverse solution over the cortical surface source space. Minimum-norm\nfamily (MNE / dSPM / sLORETA / eLORETA), all standard MNE-Python and citable; an\nLCMV beamformer is the natural alternative and is noted in the README.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport logging\n\nimport numpy as np\n\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\n\n_INVERSE_METHODS = (\"MNE\", \"dSPM\", \"sLORETA\", \"eLORETA\")\n\n\nclass InverseError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when the inverse operator or source estimate cannot be built.\"\"\"\n\n\ndef make_inverse(\n    paths: SubjectPaths,\n    info,\n    noise_cov,\n    logger: logging.Logger,\n    loose: float = 0.2,\n    depth: float = 0.8,\n):\n    \"\"\"Build and save the inverse operator.\n\n    `loose=0.2` is the standard loose-orientation constraint for a cortical\n    surface source space: dipoles are biased toward the surface normal but not\n    fixed to it. (A volume grid would instead use loose=1.0, free orientation.)\n    \"\"\"\n    import mne\n    from mne.minimum_norm import make_inverse_operator, write_inverse_operator\n\n    fwd = mne.read_forward_solution(str(paths.forward), verbose=\"ERROR\")\n\n    # A surface source space carries per-source normals and supports loose\n    # orientation; a volume one does not, so fall back to free orientation.\n    is_surface = all(s[\"type\"] == \"surf\" for s in fwd[\"src\"])\n    if not is_surface:\n        loose = 1.0\n\n    try:\n        inv = make_inverse_operator(\n            info, fwd, noise_cov, loose=loose, depth=depth, verbose=\"ERROR\",\n        )\n    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001\n        raise InverseError(\n            f\"Could not build the inverse operator: {exc}. Choose from {_INVERSE_METHODS}.\"\n        )\n\n    lambda2 = 1.0 / snr ** 2\n    stc = apply_inverse(evoked, inv, lambda2=lambda2, method=method,\n                        verbose=\"ERROR\")\n\n    paths.source_estimate.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    stc.save(str(paths.source_estimate), overwrite=True, verbose=\"ERROR\")\n    logger.info(\"Wrote %s source estimate -> %s-[lh|rh].stc\",\n                method, paths.source_estimate.name)\n\n    if not np.all(np.isfinite(stc.data)):\n        raise InverseError(\"Source estimate contains non-finite values.\")\n    return stc\n\n\ndef peak_location(stc, src, logger: logging.Logger) -> dict[str, float]:\n    \"\"\"Locate the strongest source, in mesh-world (MRI) millimetres.\n\n    Works for a surface estimate (lh+rh) and for a volume estimate. For clinical\n    localisation this peak, not the whole map, is usually the deliverable.\n    \"\"\"\n    vert_idx, t_peak = stc.get_peak(vert_as_index=True, time_as_index=False)\n\n    # Positions of the in-

```text
I'm refactoring the `_run_anatomy` function to work with the FEM route instead of the standard pipeline. I need to strip out the BEM surface generation steps, swap the coregistration approach to use mesh helpers and fit the registration directly, and replace the forward model calculation with the FEM-specific version. The function will take parameters for triangle count, conductivity values, and source position.

Now I'm realizing the parameter threading is getting unwieldy — I need to clean up the `reconstruct_sources` signature by removing the old BEM-related parameters like `os_mm`, `restrict_to_brain`, and `mindist_mm`, and add the new FEM parameters like `subsampling` and `cpus`. Rather than rename the function for clarity, I'll keep `_run_anatomy` to minimize changes in the caller, but I need to carefully update both the function signature and all the call sites to pass the right parameters through consistently.
```
</details>
