## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-21T22:37:26.657Z</sub>

C'est confirmé — voici une frame du rendu, et le **GIF de rotation 3D natif MNE** (fond noir, la vraie sortie du backend PyVista) : [stc_3d_rotation.gif](../../AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-USER-Documents-Henri-Mondor-IRM-to-MNE-python/6c11610c-cac9-4396-8398-266fd741c3e0/scratchpad/stc_3d_rotation.gif) (36 images, tour complet).

## Ton intuition est juste

> une fois qu'on a le stc, il suffit d'une fonction qui passe de SimNIBS à FreeSurfer, et MNE le traite comme une variable qu'il connaît.

Oui, **exactement**. Le `.stc` + la géométrie de surface écrite au format FreeSurfer = MNE ne voit plus aucune différence avec un sujet FreeSurfer natif. Tout le catalogue 3D marche alors :
- `stc.plot()` (ce qu'on vient de voir), `brain.save_movie()` (animation dans le temps), rotation caméra, vues médiales/latérales, plusieurs surfaces
- extraction de décours temporels par ROI, seuils, snapshots multi-temps, etc.

Et le même script **sans** `PYVISTA_OFF_SCREEN` ouvre la **fenêtre interactive** que tu peux faire tourner à la souris sur ta machine — je l'ai juste mis en offscreen ici pour capturer des images.

## La seule nuance (le point où ça se limite)

La correspondance SimNIBS→FreeSurfer que j'ai faite est **valable pour un sujet sur sa propre surface**. Le seul truc qui ne « passe » pas aussi trivialement, c'est le **morph inter-sujets vers fsaverage** : celui-ci a besoin de la *registration sphérique* FreeSurfer (`sphere.reg`), que les surfaces SimNIBS n'ont pas dans la topologie FreeSurfer. C'est pour ça que le pipeline utilise le **mapping fsaverage propre à SimNIBS** (l'option `morph_to_fsaverage`) plutôt que le morpher de surface de MNE. Une fois morphé en espace fsaverage, tu retombes sur du 100 % MNE standard (et là tu aurais le beau cerveau *gonflé* lisse).

## Une suggestion concrète

Cette « fonction pont » vit pour l'instant dans mon script de scratch. Comme c'est exactement le genre d'utilitaire réutilisable que tu décris, je peux l'**intégrer proprement au package** — par ex. un module `mri2mne/viz.py` avec :
- `write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths)` → écrit `lh/rh.white` (+ curv) depuis le `-src.fif`
- `plot_sources(paths, initial_time=..., hemi="both")` → renvoie un `Brain` MNE prêt à l'emploi (interactif ou pour screenshot/movie)

Comme ça, visualiser un sujet devient un one-liner, et c'est du code citable (MNE + SimNIBS). Tu veux que je l'ajoute au package ?

1	"""Per-subject directory layout and the stage cache.
2	
3	Every stage records completion in `status.json` together with a fingerprint of
4	its inputs. Re-running the batch therefore skips finished work, but a changed
5	input (a re-exported DICOM series, a new digitisation file) invalidates the
6	affected stage automatically instead of silently reusing stale output.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import hashlib
12	import json
13	from dataclasses import dataclass
14	from datetime import datetime, timezone
15	from pathlib import Path
16	from typing import Any
17	
18	
19	@dataclass
20	class SubjectPaths:
21	    """Resolved locations for one subject."""
22	
23	    subject: str
24	    derivatives_root: Path
25	    subjects_dir: Path
26	
27	    @property
28	    def root(self) -> Path:
29	        return self.derivatives_root / self.subject
30	
31	    @property
32	    def anat_dir(self) -> Path:
33	        return self.root / "anat"
34	
35	    @property
36	    def t1_nifti(self) -> Path:
37	        return self.anat_dir / f"{self.subject}_T1w.nii.gz"
38	
39	    @property
40	    def t1_defaced(self) -> Path:
41	        """Defaced copy for sharing. Never fed to charm: the geometric cut
42	        removes scalp and frontal bone that the segmentation needs."""
43	        return self.anat_dir / f"{self.subject}_T1w_defaced.nii.gz"
44	
45	    @property
46	    def m2m_dir(self) -> Path:
47	        """SimNIBS head-model folder. charm names it `m2m_<subid>`."""
48	        return self.root / f"m2m_{self.subject}"
49	
50	    @property
51	    def final_tissues(self) -> Path:
52	        return self.m2m_dir / "final_tissues.nii.gz"
53	
54	    @property
55	    def tissue_lut(self) -> Path:
56	        return self.m2m_dir / "final_tissues_LUT.txt"
57	
58	    # --- FreeSurfer-style layout that MNE expects ---------------------------
59	    # MNE addresses coregistration inputs through a FreeSurfer-style
60	    # subjects_dir/<subject>/bem layout; we synthesise just that folder (no
61	    # FreeSurfer is run) to hold the scalp head surface and the fiducials.
62	    @property
63	    def fs_subject_dir(self) -> Path:
64	        return self.subjects_dir / self.subject
65	
66	    @property
67	    def fs_bem_dir(self) -> Path:
68	        return self.fs_subject_dir / "bem"
69	
70	    @property
71	    def head_surface(self) -> Path:
72	        """Scalp surface (from the SimNIBS mesh) that ICP fits against."""
73	        return self.fs_bem_dir / f"{self.subject}-head.fif"
74	
75	    @property
76	    def fiducials(self) -> Path:
77	        return self.fs_bem_dir / f"{self.subject}-fiducials.fif"
78	
79	    # --- MNE outputs ---------------------------------------------------------
80	    @property
81	    def mne_dir(self) -> Path:
82	        return self.root / "mne"
83	
84	    @property
85	    def source_space(self) -> Path:
86	        """Cortical (surface) source space from the SimNIBS FEM forward."""
87	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-src.fif"
88	
89	    @property
90	    def trans(self) -> Path:
91	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-trans.fif"
92

95	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-fwd.fif"
96	
97	    @property

99	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-noise-cov.fif"
100	
101	    @property
102	    def inverse(self) -> Path:
103	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-inv.fif"
104	
105	    @property
106	    def source_estimate(self) -> Path:
107	        """MNE appends '-lh.stc' / '-rh.stc' for a surface source estimate, so
108	        this is a stem, not a complete filename."""
109	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}"
110

113	        return self.mne_dir / f"{self.subject}-ave.fif"
114

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-21T22:40:53.267Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
I see the directory structure now — the surfaces should go into the `surf` subdirectory under the FreeSurfer subject directory, which aligns perfectly with the existing layout that already has the bem directory with head and fiducials. I'm about to add a `surf_` parameter to handle this.

Writing the visualization module... I need to be careful about how vertno maps to the actual surface vertices. When we have a source estimate, the vertex indices in stc.vertices reference into the full surface via the vertno lookup, so the FreeSurfer surface we export needs to contain all the vertices that vertno points to—that's the complete set from src[0]['rr'] with its full vertex count and triangulation.

Even if someone passes a decimated source space where the number of points exceeds the number used vertices, writing out the full rr and tris is still correct because the triangulation indexes into the complete vertex set and vertno selects from it. In the SimNIBS case where np equals nuse, it's identical, but the robust approach is to always write the full geometry rather than subsetting by vertno.

For the smoothing steps in MNE's stc.plot, the surface topology from tris is used to compute adjacency, which works fine with what we're providing. Since SimNIBS doesn't provide inflated surfaces, I'll just duplicate the white geometry for those, which means inflated and white will look the same—that's acceptable and worth documenting.

With a flat curvature (all zeros), the brain renders as uniform gray without sulcal shading, which is what we're seeing. The classic colormap MNE uses by default needs curvature for binary sulci/gyri contrast, but zeros still produce valid output.

Now I'm adding a convenience function to plot sources...

Finishing the plot_sources function...

I'm also thinking about whether to add a helper function to quickly construct SubjectPaths from a derivatives directory and subject name, or just show the user how to build it manually for their external script. A convenience constructor would make it easier for them to call the plotting function from their own code. Writing the convenience wrapper... Looking at different approaches to keep the window alive in a script — the interactive session works fine, but for plain Python scripts I need to handle the Qt event loop properly. The cleanest solution seems to be using the PyVista plotter's app directly rather than trying to work around it with input() or other hacks. I'm thinking through the practical approach: setting the 3D backend to pyvistaqt and then blocking at the end of the script using a main guard to keep the window interactive until the user closes it. Adding error handling... I'll focus the viz module on four core functions: writing FreeSurfer surfaces, plotting sources interactively, opening the viewer from an output directory, and a save_views helper that generates a static montage PNG for reports and QC workflows. This keeps the scope tight while covering both interactive and headless use cases.

I should also add a `surf_dir` property to paths.py, create an example script showing how to use the viewer externally, and test the pipeline with an offscreen smoke test to validate the code works with real data. I'll set `show_traces` to default to 'auto' in plot_sources, and make sure read_source_estimate gets the stem it needs from the paths module.

Now I'm checking the file naming — MNE appends '-lh.stc' and '-rh.stc' to the stem, so when I pass 'realE2E' to read_source_estimate, it correctly finds realE2E-lh.stc and realE2E-rh.stc. The inverse.py save path already outputs the right stem, so the naming should work out.

For the surface writing, I'm putting the FreeSurfer surfaces into paths.surf_dir, which is subjects_dir/subject/surf. Since the coregistration step already created subjects_dir/subject/bem with the head.fif and fiducials, adding the surf directory keeps everything organized in the same subject folder. I can use nibabel's FreeSurfer I/O functions to write the morphed data, though I should check if MNE has a built-in surface writer that might be simpler.

For the curv file, I'll use nibabel since it has the write_morph_data function and is already a dependency of MNE. I'll wrap it in a try/except so if the writer fails, MNE can fall back to using a flat cortex without the curvature data. I also need to handle the surface parameter — if the user requests 'inflated' but I only have a white surface copy, that's fine.

Now I'm ready to write the viz.py module.
```
</details>

  "content": "\"\"\"Interactive and offscreen visualisation of the cortical source estimate.\n\nMNE's surface plotting (`stc.plot`, `mne.viz.Brain`, movies) expects a\nFreeSurfer `subjects_dir/<subject>/surf/lh.white` layout. Our cortical source\nspace is a SimNIBS *central* surface, not a FreeSurfer subject, so those files\ndo not exist on disk. Because the source space keeps its full triangulation and\nuses every vertex (`nuse == np`), the stc vertex numbering already matches the\nmesh -- so we simply write that SimNIBS mesh once in FreeSurfer surface format\nand MNE then treats the subject as a native FreeSurfer subject. The whole\n`mne.viz.Brain` toolbox (rotate-with-the-mouse window, time slider, movies,\nROI time courses) works unchanged afterwards.\n\nTypical use, from an external script on the analyst's own machine::\n\n    from mri2mne.viz import open_viewer\n    brain = open_viewer(\"D:/derivatives\", \"patient01\", initial_time=0.1)\n    # ... an interactive window opens; block until it is closed:\n    from mri2mne.viz import block_on_viewer\n    block_on_viewer()\n\nFor a headless report figure instead of a window, use ``save_views`` (it renders\noffscreen and writes a PNG).\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport logging\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\n\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\n\n# The three surface names stc.plot may ask for. SimNIBS gives us one real\n# (folded) cortical surface; we write it under every name so any `surface=`\n# choice resolves. There is no true inflated geometry (SimNIBS does not inflate),\n# so surface=\"inflated\" looks identical to \"white\" -- for a genuinely inflated,\n# smooth brain, morph to fsaverage (pipeline option `morph_to_fsaverage`) and\n# plot with subject=\"fsaverage\".\n_SURF_NAMES = (\"white\", \"pial\", \"inflated\")\n\n\nclass VizError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when the source estimate cannot be turned into a brain plot.\"\"\"\n\n\ndef _log(logger: logging.Logger | None, level: int, msg: str, *args) -> None:\n    if logger is not None:\n        logger.log(level, msg, *args)\n\n\ndef write_freesurfer_surfaces(\n    paths: SubjectPaths,\n    *,\n    source_space: str | Path | None = None,\n    overwrite: bool = False,\n    logger: logging.Logger | None = None,\n) -> Path:\n    \"\"\"Write the SimNIBS cortical mesh as FreeSurfer surfaces for MNE.\n\n    Reads the two-hemisphere surface source space (``paths.source_space`` unless\n    ``source_space`` is given) and writes ``lh/rh.{white,pial,inflated}`` plus a\n    flat ``lh/rh.curv`` into ``paths.surf_dir``. Idempotent: skips writing when\n    the files already exist unless ``overwrite`` is set.\n\n    Returns the ``subjects_dir`` to hand to MNE's ``subject=paths.subject``.\n    \"\"\"\n    import mne\n\n    src_path = Path(source_space) if source_space else paths.source_space\n    if not src_path.is_file():\n        raise VizError(\n            f\"Source space not found: {src_path}. Run the forward stage first.\"\n        )\n\n    surf_dir = paths.surf_dir\n    expected = [surf_dir / f\"{h}.{n}\" for h in (\"lh\", \"rh\") for n in _SURF_NAMES]\n    if not overwrite and all(p.exists() for p in expected):\n        _log(logger, logging.INFO, \"FreeSurfer surfaces already present in %s\", surf_dir)\n        return paths.subjects_dir\n\n    src = mne.read_source_spaces(str(src_path), verbose=\"ERROR\")\n    if len(src) != 2 or any(s.get(\"type\") != \"surf\" for s in src):\n        raise VizError(\n            \"Expected a two-hemisphere cortical (surface) source space; got \"\n            f\"{len(src)} space(s) of type \"\n            f\"{[s.get('type') for s in src]}. Volumetric estimates cannot be \"\n            \"plotted on a cortical surface.\"\n        )\n\n    surf_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    for hemi, s in zip((\"lh\", \"rh\"), src):\n        # Write the FUL

  "content": "\"\"\"Open the interactive 3D source viewer for one processed subject.\n\nThe rotate-with-the-mouse MNE window with a time slider. Point it at the output\nof a `reconstruct_sources` / batch run:\n\n    python examples/open_source_viewer.py D:/derivatives patient01\n    python examples/open_source_viewer.py D:/derivatives patient01 --time 0.1\n\nUnder the hood it writes the SimNIBS cortical mesh in FreeSurfer format (once,\ncached) and hands it to MNE's ordinary `stc.plot`, so everything MNE's Brain can\ndo -- rotate, zoom, scrub time, switch hemispheres -- is available.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport argparse\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nREPO = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]\nsys.path.insert(0, str(REPO / \"src\"))\n\nimport mne  # noqa: E402\nfrom mri2mne.viz import block_on_viewer, open_viewer  # noqa: E402\n\n\ndef main() -> int:\n    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)\n    ap.add_argument(\"output_dir\", help=\"derivatives root passed to the pipeline\")\n    ap.add_argument(\"subject\", help=\"subject id\")\n    ap.add_argument(\"--time\", type=float, default=None,\n                    help=\"initial time in seconds (default: the global peak)\")\n    ap.add_argument(\"--hemi\", default=\"both\", choices=[\"lh\", \"rh\", \"both\", \"split\"])\n    ap.add_argument(\"--surface\", default=\"white\")\n    ap.add_argument(\"--subjects-dir\", default=None,\n                    help=\"override (default: <output_dir>/subjects)\")\n    args = ap.parse_args()\n\n    mne.set_log_level(\"ERROR\")\n    mne.viz.set_3d_backend(\"pyvistaqt\")\n\n    brain = open_viewer(\n        args.output_dir, args.subject,\n        subjects_dir=args.subjects_dir,\n        initial_time=args.time, hemi=args.hemi, surface=args.surface,\n        time_viewer=True,\n    )\n    print(\"Interactive viewer open. Close the window to exit.\")\n    block_on_viewer()   # keep the window alive until the user closes it\n    return 0\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    raise SystemExit(main())\n"
}

=== head of test_config.py ===
"""Tests for configuration loading and validation.

A malformed config must fail immediately, not two hours into a charm run.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import sys
import textwrap
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] / "src"))

from mri2mne.config import ConfigError, load_config  # noqa: E402

MINIMAL = """\
paths:
  dicom_root: "D:/data/dicom"
  derivatives_root: "D:/data/deriv"
  subjects_dir: "D:/data/deriv/subjects"
  digitisation: "D:/data/dig/{subject}.elc"
"""


def write_config(tmp_path: Path, text: str) -> Path:
    path = tmp_path / "config.yaml"
    path.write_text(textwrap.dedent(text), encoding="utf-8")
    return path


def test_minimal_config_loads(tmp_path: Path):
    config = load_config(write_config(tmp_path, MINIMAL))
    assert config.paths.dicom_root == Path("D:/data/dicom")
    assert config.fem.subsampling == 10000
    assert config.fem.morph_to_fsaverage == 5
    assert config.run.continue_on_error is True
```
</details>

{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\tests\\test_viz.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Tests for the SimNIBS->FreeSurfer surface bridge used by visualisation.\n\nOnly the pure-Python bridge is exercised (no OpenGL): given a two-hemisphere\nsurface source space, the right FreeSurfer surface files must appear with the\nmesh intact, the call must be idempotent, and a volumetric space must be\nrejected rather than silently mishandled.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\nimport pytest\n\nsys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] / \"src\"))\n\nfrom mri2mne.paths import SubjectPaths  # noqa: E402\nfrom mri2mne import viz  # noqa: E402\n\n\ndef _fake_surf_hemi(n=6):\n    \"\"\"A tiny valid surface: an octahedron-ish patch (rr in metres, tris).\"\"\"\n    rr = np.array(\n        [[0, 0, 1], [1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [-1, 0, 0], [0, -1, 0], [0, 0, -1]],\n        dtype=float,\n    ) * 0.05  # ~5 cm, metres\n    tris = np.array(\n        [[0, 1, 2], [0, 2, 3], [0, 3, 4], [0, 4, 1],\n         [5, 2, 1], [5, 3, 2], [5, 4, 3], [5, 1, 4]],\n        dtype=np.int32,\n    )\n    return {\"type\": \"surf\", \"rr\": rr, \"tris\": tris, \"np\": len(rr),\n            \"ntri\": len(tris), \"nuse\": len(rr)}\n\n\ndef _paths(tmp_path: Path) -> SubjectPaths:\n    return SubjectPaths(\n        subject=\"sub01\",\n        derivatives_root=tmp_path / \"deriv\",\n        subjects_dir=tmp_path / \"deriv\" / \"subjects\",\n    )\n\n\ndef test_writes_freesurfer_surfaces(tmp_path, monkeypatch):\n    import mne\n\n    paths = _paths(tmp_path)\n    paths.source_space.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    paths.source_space.write_bytes(b\"stub\")  # existence check only\n\n    src = [_fake_surf_hemi(), _fake_surf_hemi()]\n    monkeypatch.setattr(mne, \"read_source_spaces\", lambda *a, **k: src)\n\n    subjects_dir = viz.write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths, overwrite=True)\n    assert subjects_dir == paths.subjects_dir\n\n    for hemi in (\"lh\", \"rh\"):\n        for name in (\"white\", \"pial\", \"inflated\"):\n            f = paths.surf_dir / f\"{hemi}.{name}\"\n            assert f.is_file()\n            rr, tris = mne.read_surface(str(f))\n            assert rr.shape == (6, 3)\n            assert tris.shape == (8, 3)\n            # rr was written in millimetres (0.05 m -> 50 mm)\n            assert np.isclose(np.abs(rr).max(), 50.0)\n\n\ndef test_idempotent_skip(tmp_path, monkeypatch):\n    import mne\n\n    paths = _paths(tmp_path)\n    paths.source_space.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    paths.source_space.write_bytes(b\"stub\")\n\n    calls = {\"n\": 0}\n\n    def counting_reader(*a, **k):\n        calls[\"n\"] += 1\n        return [_fake_surf_hemi(), _fake_surf_hemi()]\n\n    monkeypatch.setattr(mne, \"read_source_spaces\", counting_reader)\n\n    viz.write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths, overwrite=True)\n    assert calls[\"n\"] == 1\n    viz.write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths)          # files present -> skip\n    assert calls[\"n\"] == 1                        # reader not called again\n\n\ndef test_rejects_volumetric(tmp_path, monkeypatch):\n    import mne\n\n    paths = _paths(tmp_path)\n    paths.source_space.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    paths.source_space.write_bytes(b\"stub\")\n\n    vol = [{\"type\": \"vol\", \"rr\": np.zeros((3, 3)), \"tris\": None}]\n    monkeypatch.setattr(mne, \"read_source_spaces\", lambda *a, **k: vol)\n\n    with pytest.raises(viz.VizError):\n        viz.write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths, overwrite=True)\n\n\ndef test_missing_source_space_raises(tmp_path):\n    paths = _paths(tmp_path)  # nothing on disk\n    with pytest.raises(viz.VizError):\n        viz.write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths, overwrite=True)\n"
}

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-21T22:44:55.558Z</sub>

C'est bouclé, et tout est vérifié. `viz.py` est en place, la suite passe (**44 tests**, +4 pour le pont).

## Réponse à ta question sur la fenêtre interactive

Oui — la vraie fenêtre 3D **où tu fais tourner le cerveau à la souris + slider temporel** marche exactement comme tu l'imaginais : un script externe qui lit le stc et appelle la fonction de conversion. C'est `time_viewer=True` **sans** mode offscreen. Deux façons :

**En une ligne (script fourni)** :
```powershell
C:/Users/USER/Miniconda3/envs/irm2mne/python.exe examples/open_source_viewer.py D:/derivatives patient01 --time 0.1
```

**Ou en Python** :
```python
from mri2mne.viz import open_viewer, block_on_viewer
brain = open_viewer("D:/derivatives", "patient01", initial_time=0.1)
block_on_viewer()   # garde la fenêtre ouverte jusqu'à ce que tu la fermes
```

En session interactive (IPython/Jupyter avec le GUI Qt) la fenêtre reste vivante toute seule ; `block_on_viewer()` sert juste à ne pas fermer la fenêtre quand on lance un `.py` simple.

## Ce que contient `viz.py` ([src/mri2mne/viz.py](src/mri2mne/viz.py))

| Fonction | Rôle |
|---|---|
| `write_freesurfer_surfaces(paths)` | **le pont** : écrit le maillage SimNIBS (`-src.fif`) en `lh/rh.white` (+ pial/inflated/curv) dans `subjects_dir/<sujet>/surf`. Idempotent, cache automatiquement. |
| `plot_sources(paths, initial_time=..., hemi=...)` | écrit les surfaces si besoin, lit le stc, renvoie un `mne.viz.Brain` (interactif par défaut). |
| `open_viewer(output_dir, subject, ...)` | raccourci qui construit les `paths` standards et ouvre la fenêtre. |
| `block_on_viewer()` | bloque sur la boucle Qt pour garder la fenêtre ouverte dans un script. |
| `save_views(paths, out.png, ...)` | rendu **offscreen** (latérale/médiale/dorsale) pour un PNG de rapport, utilisable en batch headless. |

Tout repose sur les fonctions natives MNE + le maillage SimNIBS — donc **citable**, pas de code de rendu maison. J'ai testé le chemin complet sur les données réelles (surfaces écrites, montage 3-vues produit) et vérifié le skip idempotent + le rejet d'un source space volumétrique.

Deux notes honnêtes déjà dans le docstring du module :
- `surface="inflated"` = identique à `white` (SimNIBS ne gonfle pas les surfaces) ; pour le vrai cerveau lisse gonflé, passe par le morph `fsaverage` puis `subject="fsaverage"`.
- Le fond est uniforme (curv plat) — pas d'ombrage gyri/sillons, purement cosmétique.

Tu veux que j'ajoute une **section « Visualisation » au README** avec ces exemples, et/ou que je branche `save_views` dans le rapport QC par sujet (une vignette du stc dans le HTML) ?

---

### 📤 (tool results)  <sub>2026-07-21T22:50:30.796Z</sub>

<details><summary>📤 Tool result</summary>

```text
1	# IRM → MNE : analyse de sources EEG corticale (FEM SimNIBS, Windows natif)
2	
3	Pipeline complet allant d'un **IRM en DICOM** + un **enregistrement EEG** à
4	l'**estimation de sources corticale** avec MNE-Python. Piloté en Python,
5	exécutable nativement sous Windows, **sans FreeSurfer, sans WSL, sans Docker**.
6	
7	La méthode est entièrement bâtie sur des librairies **établies et citables** :
8	segmentation et forward **FEM** par **SimNIBS** (`charm`, `compute_tdcs_leadfield`,
9	`make_forward`), coregistration et inverse par **MNE-Python**. Le code de ce dépôt
10	n'est que l'orchestration entre les deux.
11	
12	Compter **~1h30 par sujet** pour `charm` + **~20-40 min** pour le leadfield FEM,
13	au lieu des 10-20 h de `recon-all`.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Entrée → sortie, en une phrase
18	
19	**On part de** l'IRM anatomique du patient en DICOM + l'enregistrement EEG (EDF
20	ou autre) + la digitalisation des électrodes. **On arrive à** l'estimation de
21	sources EEG sur le **cortex** : la localisation de l'activité mesurée, avec en
22	prime un morph vers `fsaverage` pour l'analyse de groupe.
23	
24	## Ce que le pipeline produit
25	
26	Pour chaque sujet, dans `derivatives/<sujet>/mne/` :
27	
28	| Fichier | Contenu |
29	|---|---|
30	| `<sujet>-trans.fif` | Corecalage tête ↔ IRM |
31	| `<sujet>-fwd.fif` | Forward **FEM** sur l'espace de sources **cortical** (lh+rh) |
32	| `<sujet>-noise-cov.fif` | Covariance du bruit |
33	| `<sujet>-inv.fif` | Opérateur inverse |
34	| `<sujet>-lh.stc` / `-rh.stc` | **Estimation de sources corticale** — le livrable |
35	| `<sujet>-morph.h5` | Morph vers `fsaverage` (analyse de groupe) |
36	
37	Deux niveaux d'usage :
38	
39	* **`reconstruct_sources()`** (un sujet, voir plus bas) va de l'IRM+EEG jusqu'à
40	  l'estimation de sources.
41	* **`run_pipeline.py`** (lot) enchaîne conversion → `charm` → coreg → forward FEM
42	  pour N sujets ; l'inverse (dépendant des données EEG) se fait ensuite via le
43	  wrapper.
44	
45	---
46	
47	## Architecture : deux environnements
48	
49	Le pipeline utilise **deux environnements conda** :
50	
51	* **`irm2mne`** — pilote tout (ce dépôt). N'importe **jamais** `simnibs`.
52	* **`simnibs_env`** — SimNIBS 4.6 + MNE. Exécute les étapes propres à SimNIBS,
53	  appelées en **sous-processus** par `irm2mne`.
54	
55	C'est ce qui permet d'utiliser SimNIBS et MNE dans leurs versions natives sans
56	conflit de dépendances (numpy notamment).
57	
58	### Les étapes, et la librairie derrière chacune
59	
60	| # | Étape | Fonction | Librairie |
61	|---|---|---|---|
62	| 1 | DICOM → NIfTI + anonymisation + sélection T1 | `dcm2niix`, `pydicom` | — |
63	| 2 | Segmentation + maillage FEM + surfaces corticales | `charm` | SimNIBS |
64	| 3a | Surface de scalp (pour l'ICP) | `mesh.crop_mesh` | SimNIBS |
65	| 3b | Fiduciaux sujet | `read_csv_positions` | SimNIBS |
66	| 3c | Alignement fiduciaire + ICP | `Coregistration` | MNE |
67	| 4a | Montage électrodes → sujet | `prepare_montage` | SimNIBS |
68	| 4b | Leadfield FEM (réciprocité) | `compute_tdcs_leadfield` | SimNIBS |
69	| 4c | Conversion en `mne.Forward` (+ morph fsaverage) | `make_forward` | SimNIBS |
70	| 5 | EEG : lecture, filtrage, covariance | `mne.io`, `compute_covariance` | MNE |
71	| 6 | Opérateur inverse + application | `make_inverse_operator`, `apply_inverse` | MNE |
72	
73	Repère de coordonnées : le monde du maillage SimNIBS est traité comme le repère
74	« MRI » de MNE, ce qui permet de réutiliser `Coregistration` sans conversion.
75	
76	---
77	
78	## Fonction wrapper : de l'IRM+EEG aux sources, en un appel
79	
80	```python
81	from mri2mne.wrapper import reconstruct_sources
82	
83	result = reconstruct_sources(
84	    subject="patient01",
85	    output_dir="D:/derivatives",
86	    dicom_dir="D:/dicom/patient01",          # ou t1_path="..." pour un T1 prêt
87	    eeg_file="D:/eeg/patient01.edf",         # .edf .bdf .vhdr .set .fif ...
88	    digitization="D:/dig/patient01.elc",     # positions des électrodes
89	    simnibs_bin_dir="
…[truncated 9446 chars]
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-21T22:56:51.954Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
The user is asking for a planning outline for volumetric source analysis—they want to know the setup steps, required libraries, and a rough estimate of code complexity for building an end-to-end command-line pipeline that works across Windows and WSL2, similar to what we discussed for the surface analysis. I'm trying to recall what we established earlier about the WSL2 integration challenges.

Now I'm thinking through the technical landscape for volumetric EEG source analysis. The key distinction is that volumetric source spaces use a grid of dipoles throughout the brain volume rather than just the cortical surface. For the forward model, BEM is native to MNE but requires closed surface meshes—we ran into issues with marching-cubes BEM failing to close properly, and FreeSurfer's watershed BEM is the standard but computationally expensive. SimNIBS is interesting here because it can actually compute leadfields on a volumetric grid through its FEM mesh, not just on the central gray matter surface like the typical SimNIBS-to-MNE pipeline. The question is whether we can define a volumetric source space and interpolate the leadfield across it.

I'm weighing two main approaches: using MNE's volumetric BEM setup with surfaces extracted from SimNIBS's tissue segmentation (avoiding FreeSurfer entirely and staying Windows-native), or leveraging SimNIBS's FEM directly by defining a volumetric grid of sources within gray matter and computing the leadfield at those positions.

The second option is actually cleaner—I can reuse the reciprocity approach already in our code, just swapping the target points from surface nodes to a volumetric grid. This keeps everything Windows-native, citable, and builds on what we've already written.

Let me check how SimNIBS structures this. The `compute_tdcs_leadfield` function generates an HDF5 file with the E-field sampled at specific points, and then `make_forward` converts that into an MNE-compatible forward model using whatever source space you provide—surface or volumetric.

For volumetric, I'd need to define a grid of source positions in MRI space (masked to gray matter) and interpolate the FEM leadfield at those positions using SimNIBS's interpolation tools or by evaluating the leadfield at custom point locations.

Let me organize this into clear options with honest tradeoffs for the user. Adapting the leadfield extraction to work with volumetric grid points and reverting the inverse solver to handle volumes (pulling from earlier git history) would add maybe 200–400 lines plus tests, though there's a risk that SimNIBS's public MNE integration is surface-focused and I might need to drop down to lower-level APIs to evaluate the E-field at arbitrary points. Route 2 explores a fully Windows-native MNE BEM approach instead, using `setup_volume_source_space` with three BEM surfaces derived from the charm segmentation rather than FreeSurfer.

The earlier marching-cubes attempt ran into non-manifold mesh issues, but I could leverage SimNIBS's own surface mesh outputs or use `pymeshfix` to clean and close them—adding libraries like pymeshfix and scikit-image, though surface repair is notoriously finicky and less standard to justify. Route 3 circles back to WSL2 with FreeSurfer and MNE, using either the full recon-all pipeline or just the BEM watershed step.

The watershed approach is faster—just minutes instead of 6-10 hours—since `mne.bem.make_watershed_bem` wraps FreeSurfer's `mri_watershed` and only needs a T1-conformed image. The catch is FreeSurfer still has to be installed in WSL2, and then I'd run the BEM generation either within WSL or pull the results back to the host.

I could build an orchestration layer that calls WSL via subprocess (similar to how we handled the SimNIBS environment), making it transparent to the user. That's moderate complexity.

Route 4 with FSL and osl-ephys is another option but doesn't seem to offer a clear advantage over the watershed approach, and it adds more setup overhead. Given the user's constraints—Windows-native preference, citable methods, and avoiding long runtimes—Route 1 with SimNIBS FEM volumetric looks like the strongest recommendation.

The key insight is that FEM naturally produces volumetric outputs; we're currently discarding that by restricting to surfaces. We already have most of the machinery in place, so it's really just a different source space and inverse problem. I should present the WSL2 + FreeSurfer BEM route as a fallback if they want the textbook volumetric BEM approach.

But I need to be honest about the risk here: I should verify that SimNIBS can actually extract leadfields at arbitrary volume points through its API. This probably needs a quick de-risking spike to confirm it's feasible before committing to it as the main recommendation.

Actually, thinking through the SimNIBS architecture—the leadfield HDF5 stores the E-field per electrode at every mesh element or node in the gray matter (or a specified ROI). If we set the ROI to the whole gray matter volume, we get E-field throughout the volume, and a volumetric source space is just sampling from that. The `TDCSLEADFIELD` function has a `tissues` parameter that computes the leadfield at tetrahedron centers in those tissues, which is naturally volumetric. So Route 1 is actually quite feasible and close to native SimNIBS functionality.

To be more precise, `compute_tdcs_leadfield` with `map_to_surf=False` gives the leadfield directly at the GM tetrahedra centers—that's a volumetric leadfield right there. When `map_to_surf=True`, it maps to the middle GM surface instead. So volumetric extraction is essentially flipping that flag and building a volume source space from the tetra centers or a regular grid. This approach is very reusable and the FEM solve stays identical; we're just changing how we extract and organize the results.

For the concrete implementation, I'm looking at roughly 100-150 lines for the volumetric extraction helpers, 80-120 lines for building the volume source space (either tetra-center based or a masked regular grid), 60-100 lines for the volumetric inverse branch (which I can largely recover from the git history), around 80 lines for visualization using nilearn or MRI overlays, and another 150 lines for config plumbing and tests.

The WSL2 route would be heavier—about 100 lines for WSL detection and subprocess wrapping, 100 for FreeSurfer BEM invocation through WSL, 150 for volume source space and BEM forward modeling, 150 for inverse and visualization, and 200 for setup docs and tests. ```text
1	"""Standalone helper that runs inside SimNIBS' own Python (simnibs_env).
2	
3	The pipeline (in the irm2mne env) invokes this via subprocess because it needs
4	both `simnibs` and `mne`, which live together only in the SimNIBS environment.
5	It must therefore import nothing from the rest of mri2mne -- only simnibs, mne
6	and numpy.
7	
8	Contract (all paths passed as JSON on argv[1]):
9	    {
10	      "m2m_dir": ...,          # charm output
11	      "fem_dir": ...,          # clean output dir for the leadfield
12	      "montage_csv": ...,      # where the SimNIBS montage CSV is written
13	      "info_fif": ...,         # mne Info/raw/evoked with the EEG channels
14	      "trans_fif": ...,        # head<->mri (mesh world) transform
15	      "out_fwd": ...,          # where to write -fwd.fif
16	      "out_src": ...,          # where to write -src.fif
17	      "subsampling": 10000,    # cortical source points per hemisphere (or null)
18	      "cpus": 4,
19	      "morph_to_fsaverage": 5  # or null
20	    }
21	
22	The electrode montage is built with SimNIBS' own `prepare_montage`, which maps
23	the MNE head-coordinate electrodes into subject (mesh) space -- no bespoke
24	coordinate handling here. Prints a JSON result dict on the last stdout line.
25	"""
26	
27	import json
28	import shutil
29	import sys
30	from pathlib import Path
31	
32	
33	def _shim_mne_for_simnibs(mne) -> None:
34	    """Restore MNE private-API names SimNIBS 4.6 expects at their old location.
35	
36	    SimNIBS' eeg module calls `mne.source_space._complete_source_space_info`.
37	    In MNE >= 1.8 that function still exists but moved into the
38	    `mne.source_space._source_space` submodule and is no longer re-exported at
39	    the package level. We alias it back -- pointing at the same function, not
40	    reimplementing it -- so the SimNIBS->MNE bridge runs on current MNE.
41	    """
42	    # Newer MNE lazy-loads submodules, so the private ones SimNIBS reaches into
43	    # are not accessible until explicitly imported. Import them, then restore
44	    # the one name that also moved location. These point at the same functions
45	    # MNE still ships -- no behaviour is reimplemented.
46	    import importlib
47	
48	    for sub in (
49	        "mne.source_space._source_space",
50	        "mne.forward._make_forward",
51	        "mne.forward._compute_forward",
52	        "mne.morph",
53	        "mne.transforms",
54	    ):
55	        importlib.import_module(sub)
56	
57	    import mne.source_space
58	
59	    if not hasattr(mne.source_space, "_complete_source_space_info"):
60	        from mne.source_space import _source_space as _ss
61	
62	        mne.source_space._complete_source_space_info = _ss._complete_source_space_info
63	
64	
65	def main() -> int:
66	    cfg = json.loads(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
67	
68	    import mne
69	    import numpy as np
70	
71	    _shim_mne_for_simnibs(mne)
72	
73	    from simnibs.eeg import forward as sim_fwd
74	    from simnibs.eeg import utils_mne
75	
76	    mne.set_log_level("ERROR")
77	
78	    fem_dir = Path(cfg["fem_dir"])
79	    # SimNIBS refuses to overwrite an existing leadfield group in the hdf5, so
80	    # the FEM directory must be clean before every run.
81	    if fem_dir.exists():
82	        shutil.rmtree(fem_dir)
83	    fem_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
84	
85	    # --- Montage: SimNIBS' own head->subject electrode transform -----------
86	    utils_mne.prepare_montage(cfg["montage_csv"], cfg["info_fif"], cfg["trans_fif"])
87	
88	    # --- FEM leadfield (the slow part) -------------------------------------
89	    leadfield = sim_fwd.compute_tdcs_leadfield(
90	        cfg["m2m_dir"],
91	        str(fem_dir),
92	        cfg["montage_csv"],
93	        subsampling=cfg.get("subsampling"),
94	        point_electrodes=True,
95	        run_kwargs=dict(save_mat=False, cpus=int(cfg.get("cpus", 1))),
96	    )
97	
98	    # --- Convert to an MNE forward -----------------------------------------
99	    info 
…[truncated 1704 chars]
```
</details>

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T11:27:28.182Z</sub>

J'ai ce qu'il me faut (le helper montre que notre FEM passe par `compute_tdcs_leadfield(subsampling=…)` + `make_forward`, tous deux orientés **surface**). Voici le panorama complet pour le **volumique**, sans écrire de code pour l'instant.

## Ce que « volumique » implique concrètement

Un espace de sources **volumique** = une grille 3D de dipôles remplissant le cerveau (ex. 5 mm, masquée à la matière grise), au lieu de la surface corticale. La sortie devient une `VolSourceEstimate` (`-vl.stc`), visualisable en **overlay sur l'IRM** (coupes), pas sur une surface. Il faut donc changer 3 choses : l'espace de sources, le forward, et l'inverse (+ la viz).

Il y a essentiellement **3 routes**. Je te les donne avec setup / librairies / estimation de code, puis ma reco.

---

## Route A — FEM SimNIBS volumique, **100 % Windows, SANS WSL** ⭐ (recommandée)

L'idée-clé : **notre FEM résout déjà tout le volume**. Aujourd'hui on *jette* le volume en projetant le leadfield sur la surface corticale centrale (`subsampling` + `make_forward`). En volumique, on évalue le même leadfield sur une **grille de points dans la matière grise**. La physique (réciprocité FEM) est identique — c'est la même méthode citable, on change juste où on échantillonne.

- **Setup :** rien de neuf. SimNIBS + MNE sont déjà installés, `charm` tourne déjà.
- **Librairies :** identiques (`simnibs`, `mne`, `numpy`, `nibabel`). Pas de FSL, pas de FreeSurfer, pas de WSL.
- **Le risque honnête :** le *pont MNE publié* de SimNIBS (`sim_fwd.make_forward`) est **surface-only**. Extraire le leadfield sur des points de volume demande de descendre d'un cran dans l'API SimNIBS (configurer le leadfield pour l'enregistrer sur les tétraèdres de matière grise — son mode natif quand on ne demande pas l'interpolation surfacique — puis lire le HDF5 et l'interpoler sur la grille). C'est un peu plus de « glue » maison que la route surfacique, donc à **dé-risquer par un spike** d'une demi-journée, exactement comme on l'avait fait pour le FEM surfacique.
- **Estimation de code : ~500–700 lignes nettes** + le spike :
  - extraction leadfield volumique dans le helper : ~120–180
  - constructeur d'espace de sources volumique (grille masquée à la GM depuis `final_tissues` de charm) : ~80–120
  - `inverse.py` branche volumique (`VolSourceEstimate`, `loose=1`, `-vl.stc`) : ~60–100 — **en grande partie récupérable de l'historique git** (on l'avait avant le pivot)
  - viz volumique (overlay IRM via `nilearn`/MNE) : ~80
  - config/wrapper/pipeline + tests : ~150–200

---

## Route B — BEM volumique MNE, Windows natif, SANS WSL (celle qu'on avait abandonnée)

Volumique MNE standard : `setup_volume_source_space` + un BEM 3 couches. Le BEM exige **3 surfaces fermées** (scalp, crâne externe, crâne interne). Sans FreeSurfer, on les fabriquerait par marching-cubes sur la segmentation charm — **c'est précisément ce qui échouait** (surfaces non-manifold, non fermées). Récupérable avec `pymeshfix`/`pyvista`, mais fragile et moins citable.

- **Setup :** rien (éventuellement `pymeshfix`). **Librairies :** `mne`, `nibabel`, `scikit-image`, `pymeshfix`.
- **Risque :** le whack-a-mole de fermeture de surfaces déjà rencontré. **Non recommandée.**
- **Code :** ~400–600 lignes mais avec un vrai risque de fiabilité.

---

## Route C — **WSL2 + FreeSurfer**, BEM volumique « manuel scolaire »

FreeSurfer (dans WSL2) génère les surfaces BEM, puis MNE fait le forward BEM volumique + l'inverse. C'est la route BEM **la plus reconnue des reviewers**.

**Point crucial qui lève ta crainte des 10-20 h :** pour du BEM volumique tu n'as **pas** besoin de `recon-all` (6–10 h). Tu as besoin de `mne watershed_bem`, qui appelle `mri_watershed` de FreeSurfer → **quelques minutes**. Le recon-all complet n'est nécessaire que pour les surfaces corticales/atlas, pas pour le BEM volumique.

- **Setup (la partie lourde) :**
  1. `wsl --install` (WSL2 + Ubuntu)
  2. Installer **FreeSurfer** dans WSL (~10 Go, + un fichier de licence gratuit à demander, `export FREESURFER_HOME`, sourcer `SetUpFreeSurfer.sh`)
  3. Un env Python+MNE, soit dans WSL, soit rester côté Windows en traduisant les chemins
  4. Gérer la **traduction de chemins** Windows ↔ WSL (`C:\…` ↔ `/mnt/c/…`)
- **Librairies :** FreeSurfer (Linux), `mne`, `nibabel`.
- **Les vraies complications WSL** (ce qu'on avait établi) : install FreeSurfer + licence, traduction de chemins, pas de GL/GUI depuis WSL (sans importance ici : notre pipeline est CLI et fait la coreg par ICP), et lenteur disque sur `/mnt/c`. Aucune n'est bloquante — c'est du surcoût de setup. Et **SimNIBS tourne nativement sous Windows**, donc WSL n'est requis *que* si on choisit la route FreeSurfer/FSL.
- **Estimation de code : ~700–900 lignes :**
  - pont WSL (détection, traduction de chemins, `wsl.exe -e bash -lc "…"` en subprocess — calqué sur notre pattern `simnibs_env`) : ~120–150
  - génération des surfaces BEM via watershed dans WSL : ~120
  - espace de sources volumique + forward BEM (MNE natif) : ~150
  - inverse + viz volumique : ~150
  - config/wrapper/preflight (vérifier WSL + FreeSurfer présents) + tests + docs : ~200

*(Route D — WSL2 + FSL/osl-ephys : setup encore plus lourd, pas mieux que C pour notre besoin. Je l'écarte sauf si tu veux spécifiquement l'écosystème OSL.)*

---

## Synthèse

| Route | WSL2 ? | Setup nouveau | Citabilité | Code net | Risque |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** FEM SimNIBS volumique | ❌ non | rien | élevée (même méthode qu'actuel) | ~500–700 | API volume SimNIBS à dé-risquer |
| **B** BEM sans FreeSurfer | ❌ non | pymeshfix | moyenne | ~400–600 | **élevé** (fermeture surfaces) |
| **C** WSL2 + FreeSurfer BEM | ✅ oui | FreeSurfer ~10 Go + licence | très élevée (standard) | ~700–900 | setup WSL, sinon faible |

**Ma reco : Route A.** Elle réutilise tout ce qu'on a bâti, reste 100 % Windows, garde la méthode FEM citable, et l'inverse volumique est en partie récupérable de l'historique. Le seul inconnu (extraction leadfield volumique) se tranche par un petit spike, comme pour le surfacique — qui, lui, avait marché.

Si tu veux le BEM volumique que tout reviewer reconnaît au premier coup d'œil, **Route C** est le repli propre, avec la bonne nouvelle que c'est du watershed (minutes), pas du recon-all (heures).

Quelle route veux-tu que je creuse ?

I'm checking the current state of my WSL Ubuntu setup and seeing that FreeSurfer isn't installed yet—I'll need to get that set up along with the necessary neuroimaging tools, starting with the FreeSurfer installation which is a fairly large download.

The key architectural decision is whether to run MNE in WSL or keep it on Windows. Option 1 makes more sense: keep MNE running on Windows (in the existing irm2mne environment) and use WSL only as a subprocess to run FreeSurfer's mri_watershed for generating the BEM surfaces, then pass those surfaces back to Windows MNE for the forward and inverse computations. This minimizes the WSL footprint and reuses what's already set up, with just some path translation needed between the two systems.

For the FreeSurfer installation itself, I'll need to download the Ubuntu package (~5GB download, ~10GB installed) and register for a license from the FreeSurfer website. So the volume source space can work with just the BEM surfaces without needing a full FreeSurfer reconstruction — `make_watershed_bem` is the specific tool that extracts those surfaces from the T1 image, which is where FreeSurfer comes in. Now I'm sketching out the implementation for Route C: a WSL bridge module that detects and communicates with WSL while keeping MNE on Windows.

The bridge needs to handle path translation between Windows and Linux formats, execute FreeSurfer commands through WSL, and verify FreeSurfer is installed. For the BEM surface generation, I'd create a minimal FreeSurfer subject directory structure with the conformed T1 image, then run the watershed algorithm to extract the surfaces. the `mri_watershed` command with those flags produces the four surface files that `make_watershed_bem` would generate. Then for the Windows MNE workflow, I'd build the BEM model from those three skull/skin surfaces with conductivity values, set up a volume source space bounded by the inner skull, and create the forward solution — but I'm realizing I need to figure out whether we still need to run the charm pipeline or if we can reuse the coregistration transform we already have. we can pull MNI fiducials from fsaverage if we have the Talairach transform from recon-all, or use the SimNIBS fiducials we're already extracting, or estimate them from the scalp surface itself. This gets complicated though—Route C is really a different pipeline altogether, not just swapping out the head model. I'm thinking through two variants: C1 keeps just the FreeSurfer BEM without SimNIBS, using the watershed scalp surface and fiducials for coregistration into an MNE volume BEM.  C2 keeps SimNIBS for the scalp and fiducials but adds FreeSurfer's inner and outer skull surfaces. C1 is cleaner and more citable as a standalone volumetric pipeline, but it means duplicating the coregistration and fiducials logic. The real issue is that without recon-all, we don't have the Talairach transform that MNE normally uses to get fiducials from fsaverage, so I need to figure out how to handle that for a watershed-only subject. I could also run just the talairach registration steps from FreeSurfer separately to get the fiducials without a full recon-all, but that still requires FreeSurfer to be installed with a valid license. The real blocker here is that I need to report back what's actually available in their environment—FreeSurfer isn't set up, they don't have a license yet, and MNE isn't in the WSL—so before I start building anything substantial, they need to handle those prerequisites first. prompt for their password, which I can't handle in a non-interactive shell. They'll need to run the sudo install themselves. For the license, they must register on FreeSurfer's site since I can't create accounts or handle their personal data. So my approach is to verify what's missing, give them the exact setup commands and registration link, then start building the parts that don't depend on FreeSurfer being installed yet—like the WSL bridge module and scaffolding—while they handle the prerequisites.

Let me also test whether sudo is passwordless in their Ubuntu environment and check if FreeSurfer is available through apt, since that would simplify the installation path.
```

{
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\wsl.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"Bridge to run Linux-only tools (FreeSurfer) from the Windows pipeline via WSL2.\n\nSame design as the SimNIBS subprocess bridge: the pipeline stays in the Windows\n`irm2mne` environment and shells out to a second world -- here a WSL2 distro --\nfor the one tool that only exists there. This module owns everything WSL: is it\ninstalled, path translation Windows<->WSL, running a command, and checking that\nFreeSurfer is reachable inside the distro.\n\nNothing here imports mne or simnibs; it is pure process/path plumbing so it can\nbe unit-tested on any machine (the translation helpers) and smoke-tested wherever\nWSL is present (the runners).\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport logging\nimport re\nimport subprocess\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass\nfrom pathlib import Path, PureWindowsPath\n\n\nclass WslError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when WSL is unavailable or a command inside it fails.\"\"\"\n\n\n@dataclass\nclass WslResult:\n    \"\"\"Outcome of a command run inside WSL.\"\"\"\n\n    returncode: int\n    stdout: str\n    stderr: str\n\n    @property\n    def ok(self) -> bool:\n        return self.returncode == 0\n\n\ndef _wsl_exe() -> str:\n    # wsl.exe is always on PATH on a WSL-enabled Windows; keep it overridable in\n    # tests by monkeypatching this function rather than hard-coding the string.\n    return \"wsl.exe\"\n\n\ndef to_wsl_path(win_path: str | Path) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Translate a Windows path to its WSL `/mnt/<drive>/...` form.\n\n    Pure-Python (no `wslpath` call) so it is fast and testable offline:\n    ``C:\\\\Users\\\\me\\\\t1.nii`` -> ``/mnt/c/Users/me/t1.nii``. UNC and\n    already-POSIX paths are passed through unchanged.\n    \"\"\"\n    s = str(win_path)\n    if s.startswith(\"/\"):\n        return s  # already a POSIX path\n    pw = PureWindowsPath(s)\n    drive = pw.drive  # e.g. \"C:\"\n    if not re.match(r\"^[A-Za-z]:$\", drive):\n        raise WslError(\n            f\"Cannot translate {s!r} to a WSL path: no drive letter. Use an \"\n            \"absolute Windows path.\"\n        )\n    rest = \"/\".join(pw.parts[1:])  # parts[0] is 'C:\\\\'\n    return f\"/mnt/{drive[0].lower()}/{rest}\"\n\n\ndef from_wsl_path(wsl_path: str) -> Path:\n    \"\"\"Translate a `/mnt/<drive>/...` WSL path back to a Windows `Path`.\"\"\"\n    m = re.match(r\"^/mnt/([A-Za-z])/(.*)$\", wsl_path)\n    if not m:\n        raise WslError(f\"{wsl_path!r} is not a translatable /mnt/<drive>/ path.\")\n    drive, rest = m.group(1).upper(), m.group(2).replace(\"/\", \"\\\\\")\n    return Path(f\"{drive}:\\\\{rest}\")\n\n\ndef is_available(distro: str | None = None) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"True if WSL runs a trivial command (optionally in a named distro).\"\"\"\n    try:\n        res = run(\"true\", distro=distro, check=False, timeout=60)\n        return res.ok\n    except WslError:\n        return False\n\n\ndef run(\n    command: str,\n    *,\n    distro: str | None = None,\n    login_shell: bool = True,\n    check: bool = True,\n    timeout: float | None = None,\n    logger: logging.Logger | None = None,\n) -> WslResult:\n    \"\"\"Run a bash `command` string inside WSL and capture its output.\n\n    ``login_shell`` sources the user's profile (so FreeSurfer's environment,\n    set up in ``~/.bashrc``/``SetUpFreeSurfer.sh``, is present). Raises\n    ``WslError`` on failure when ``check`` is set.\n    \"\"\"\n    argv = [_wsl_exe()]\n    if distro:\n        argv += [\"-d\", distro]\n    flags = \"-lc\" if login_shell else \"-c\"\n    argv += [\"-e\", \"bash\", flags, command]\n\n    if logger is not None:\n        logger.debug(\"WSL run: %s\", command)\n\n    try:\n        proc = subprocess.run(\n            argv,\n            capture_output=True,\n            text=True,\n            encoding=\"utf-8\",\n            errors=\"replace\",\n            timeout=timeout,\n        )\n    e
…[truncated 3308 chars]

  "content": "\"\"\"Tests for the WSL bridge -- the parts that need no running WSL.\n\nPath translation is pure Python; the FreeSurfer probe is tested by feeding\n`wsl.run` synthetic output so the parsing is checked without a live distro.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport sys\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport pytest\n\nsys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] / \"src\"))\n\nfrom mri2mne import wsl  # noqa: E402\n\n\n@pytest.mark.parametrize(\n    \"win,expected\",\n    [\n        (r\"C:\\Users\\USER\\t1.nii.gz\", \"/mnt/c/Users/USER/t1.nii.gz\"),\n        (r\"D:\\data\\sub01\", \"/mnt/d/data/sub01\"),\n        (r\"E:\\a b\\c\", \"/mnt/e/a b/c\"),\n        (\"/already/posix\", \"/already/posix\"),\n    ],\n)\ndef test_to_wsl_path(win, expected):\n    assert wsl.to_wsl_path(win) == expected\n\n\ndef test_to_wsl_path_rejects_relative():\n    with pytest.raises(wsl.WslError):\n        wsl.to_wsl_path(r\"relative\\path\")\n\n\ndef test_from_wsl_path_roundtrip():\n    assert str(wsl.from_wsl_path(\"/mnt/d/data/sub01\")) == r\"D:\\data\\sub01\"\n    with pytest.raises(wsl.WslError):\n        wsl.from_wsl_path(\"/home/user/thing\")  # not under /mnt/<drive>\n\n\ndef _fake_run(stdout):\n    return lambda *a, **k: wsl.WslResult(0, stdout, \"\")\n\n\ndef test_check_freesurfer_present(monkeypatch):\n    out = (\n        \"HOME=/usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1\\n\"\n        \"VER=freesurfer-linux-ubuntu22_amd64-7.4.1\\n\"\n        \"/usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1/bin/mri_convert\\n\"\n        \"/usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1/bin/mri_watershed\\n\"\n        \"LICENSE_OK\\n\"\n    )\n    monkeypatch.setattr(wsl, \"run\", _fake_run(out))\n    info = wsl.check_freesurfer()\n    assert info.present and info.has_license\n    assert info.home == \"/usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1\"\n    assert info.missing == []\n\n\ndef test_check_freesurfer_absent(monkeypatch):\n    monkeypatch.setattr(wsl, \"run\", _fake_run(\"HOME=\\nVER=\\n\"))\n    info = wsl.check_freesurfer()\n    assert not info.present\n    assert not info.has_license\n    assert set(info.missing) == {\"mri_convert\", \"mri_watershed\"}\n\n\ndef test_check_freesurfer_no_license(monkeypatch):\n    out = (\n        \"HOME=/opt/freesurfer\\n\"\n        \"VER=\\n\"\n        \"/opt/freesurfer/bin/mri_convert\\n\"\n        \"/opt/freesurfer/bin/mri_watershed\\n\"\n    )\n    monkeypatch.setattr(wsl, \"run\", _fake_run(out))\n    info = wsl.check_freesurfer()\n    assert info.present            # binaries there\n    assert not info.has_license    # but no license.txt\n    assert \"license.txt not found\" in info.describe()\n"
}

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T11:37:33.057Z</sub>

Le pont WSL est **construit et testé** (9 tests, traduction de chemins + commande réelle + probe FreeSurfer). Fondation posée. ✅

Maintenant le blocage critique : **FreeSurfer et sa licence**, que toi seul peux mettre en place (sudo + inscription). Voici exactement quoi faire.

## Étape 1 — Licence FreeSurfer (gratuite, ~2 min)

1. Va sur **https://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu/registration.html**
2. Remplis le formulaire → tu reçois **`license.txt`** par email.
3. Garde ce fichier, on le placera à l'étape 2.

*(Je ne peux pas le faire à ta place : ça crée un compte avec tes données personnelles — c'est à toi.)*

## Étape 2 — Installer FreeSurfer dans Ubuntu (WSL)

Ouvre un terminal **Ubuntu** (menu Démarrer → « Ubuntu ») et lance :

```bash
cd ~
# ~2,5 Go de téléchargement
wget https://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu/pub/dist/freesurfer/7.4.1/freesurfer_ubuntu22-7.4.1_amd64.deb
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y ./freesurfer_ubuntu22-7.4.1_amd64.deb   # tire les dépendances
```

Puis place la licence (adapte le chemin où est ton `license.txt`, visible depuis WSL via `/mnt/c/...`) :

```bash
sudo cp /mnt/c/Users/USER/Downloads/license.txt /usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1/license.txt
```

Enfin, rends FreeSurfer actif à chaque shell (c'est ce que mon pont lit) :

```bash
echo 'export FREESURFER_HOME=/usr/local/freesurfer/7.4.1' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

Vérif rapide côté WSL : `mri_watershed --help` doit répondre, et `echo $FREESURFER_HOME` afficher le chemin.

## Ce que je fais dès que c'est en place

Tu me dis « c'est bon », je relance `check_freesurfer()` pour confirmer (présent + licence), puis j'enchaîne, **sans autre action de ta part** :

- **Tâche 8** — helper watershed : je conforme le T1 (`mri_convert`) et lance `mri_watershed` dans WSL → 3 surfaces BEM (inner/outer skull + scalp), rapatriées côté Windows. Je le dé-risque sur ton vrai T1 clinique déjà téléchargé (quelques minutes, pas d'heures).
- **Tâche 9** — forward BEM volumique + inverse (MNE côté Windows) : `make_bem_solution`, `setup_volume_source_space` (grille masquée par l'inner skull), forward avec le `trans` de coreg, inverse volumétrique (`-vl.stc`) + viz overlay IRM.

Deux notes :
- **RAM** : 7,4 Go suffisent pour watershed. Si un jour tu veux du recon-all complet, il faudra bumper la mémoire WSL (`.wslconfig`) — mais pour le BEM volumique on n'en a pas besoin.
- Le téléchargement `.deb` est le plus long ; le reste est rapide.

Lance l'install quand tu veux et dis-moi — je prends le relais immédiatement.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T11:48:59.725Z</sub>

Ah, c'est le grief classique **FreeSurfer .deb vs Ubuntu 24.04** : le paquet `.deb` déclare `libc6 < 2.36` et `libncurses5`, or 24.04 a glibc 2.39 et n'a plus `libncurses5`. C'est une contrainte **d'emballage** du `.deb`, pas une vraie incompatibilité — les binaires FreeSurfer tournent très bien sur glibc 2.39.

**La solution : le tarball au lieu du `.deb`**, qui n'a aucune métadonnée apt et contourne donc le solveur de dépendances. Rien n'a été installé par la tentative `.deb`, donc pas de nettoyage — enchaîne directement :

```bash
cd ~
# Tarball (pas de .deb) — plus gros mais aucune dépendance apt
wget https://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu/pub/dist/freesurfer/7.4.1/freesurfer-linux-ubuntu22_amd64-7.4.1.tar.gz

# Extrait vers /usr/local -> crée /usr/local/freesurfer
sudo tar -C /usr/local -xzf freesurfer-linux-ubuntu22_amd64-7.4.1.tar.gz
```

Ensuite la licence (adapte le chemin de ton `license.txt`) et l'environnement — **note que `FREESURFER_HOME` est maintenant `/usr/local/freesurfer`** (sans le `/7.4.1`) :

```bash
sudo cp /mnt/c/Users/USER/Downloads/license.txt /usr/local/freesurfer/license.txt

echo 'export FREESURFER_HOME=/usr/local/freesurfer' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'source $FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

Puis vérifie que les binaires dont on a besoin trouvent leurs libs (le seul risque résiduel sur 24.04 étant `libncurses.so.5`) :

```bash
echo $FREESURFER_HOME
ldd $FREESURFER_HOME/bin/mri_watershed | grep -i 'not found' || echo "deps OK"
ldd $FREESURFER_HOME/bin/mri_convert  | grep -i 'not found' || echo "deps OK"
```

- Si les deux affichent **`deps OK`** → parfait, dis-moi et je continue (tâches 8 et 9).
- Si ça liste `libncurses.so.5 => not found` (ou `libtinfo.so.5`), colle-moi la sortie : c'est un fix connu en 2 lignes (symlinks `libncurses.so.6`→`.5` / `libtinfo.so.6`→`.5`), je te le donnerai précisément.

Le tarball est plus lourd que le `.deb` (~5-7 Go), donc le téléchargement est la seule vraie attente. Le reste est immédiat.

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T12:12:58.069Z</sub>

<details><summary>💭 Chain of thought</summary>

```text
FREESURFER_HOME isn't being set because ~/.bashrc likely has an early return statement for non-interactive shells that prevents the FreeSurfer initialization lines from executing. Ubuntu's default bashrc checks if the shell is interactive and returns early if it isn't, which would skip over any FreeSurfer configuration.

The issue is that `bash -lc` runs as a login shell but in non-interactive mode, so the bashrc early-return check triggers before reaching the FreeSurfer lines appended at the end. This means the environment variables are only set during interactive shell sessions, not in the non-interactive bash calls used by the WSL bridge. The earlier "No such file or directory" error suggests the user did add the FreeSurfer sourcing line to bashrc, but it failed because the file path wasn't valid when that line actually tried to execute.

The real fix is to either explicitly source FreeSurfer in the bridge's bash calls, or place the FreeSurfer setup in ~/.profile instead of bashrc, since login shells read ~/.profile even when non-interactive. Let me verify whether the lines are actually in bashrc and check how the early-return logic works, then decide between these approaches. Instead of relying on shell configuration files, I should have the FreeSurfer helper explicitly set up the environment within each command it runs — exporting FREESURFER_HOME and sourcing the setup script directly as part of the command string. This way the subprocess bridge doesn't depend on .bashrc or .profile at all, which is cleaner and more reliable. I'll go with the pipeline-explicit approach since it's more portable and doesn't rely on the user's shell configuration being correct. I need to update `check_freesurfer()` to detect or accept `FREESURFER_HOME` and source `SetUpFreeSurfer.sh` directly.   "new_string": "# Binaries the volumetric BEM route actually needs (watershed, not recon-all).\n_REQUIRED_BINS = (\"mri_convert\", \"mri_watershed\")\n\n# Where FreeSurfer may live, tried in order. A user-home install avoids sudo.\n_FS_HOME_CANDIDATES = (\"$HOME/freesurfer\", \"/usr/local/freesurfer\")\n\n\ndef freesurfer_setup_prefix(freesurfer_home: str | None = None) -> str:\n    \"\"\"A bash snippet that puts FreeSurfer on PATH, for prefixing commands.\n\n    We source ``SetUpFreeSurfer.sh`` explicitly rather than relying on the\n    user's ``~/.bashrc``: Ubuntu's default .bashrc returns early for\n    non-interactive shells (which is exactly what ``bash -lc`` is), so the\n    FreeSurfer lines appended there never run under this bridge.\n    \"\"\"\n    if freesurfer_home:\n        pick = f'export FREESURFER_HOME=\"{freesurfer_home}\"; '\n    else:\n        cands = \" \".join(f'\"{c}\"' for c in _FS_HOME_CANDIDATES)\n        pick = (\n            f\"for H in {cands}; do \"\n            '[ -f \"$H/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh\" ] && export FREESURFER_HOME=\"$H\" && break; '\n            \"done; \"\n        )\n    return pick + 'source \"$FREESURFER_HOME/SetUpFreeSurfer.sh\" >/dev/null 2>&1; '\n\n\ndef run_freesurfer(\n    command: str,\n    *,\n    freesurfer_home: str | None = None,\n    distro: str | None = None,\n    check: bool = True,\n    timeout: float | None = None,\n    logger: logging.Logger | None = None,\n) -> WslResult:\n    \"\"\"Run `command` with FreeSurfer's environment sourced first.\"\"\"\n    return run(\n        freesurfer_setup_prefix(freesurfer_home) + command,\n        distro=distro,\n        check=check,\n        timeout=timeout,\n        logger=logger,\n    )\n\n\ndef check_freesurfer(\n    distro: str | None = None,\n    freesurfer_home: str | None = None,\n    logger: logging.Logger | None = None,\n) -> FreeSurferInfo:\n    \"\"\"Probe the distro for a usable, licensed FreeSurfer (watershed subset).\"\"\"\n    try:\n        res = run(\n            freesurfer_setup_prefix(freesurfer_home)\n            + \"echo \\\"HOME=$FREESURFER_HOME\\\"; Both the charm workflow and FreeSurfer's conform step work from the same T1 image, just in different coordinate systems — charm stays in the scanner's native space while FreeSurfer creates its own 256³ 1mm volume with tkrRAS coordinates. {
  "file_path": "C:\\Users\\USER\\Documents\\Henri_Mondor\\IRM_to_MNE_python\\src\\mri2mne\\freesurfer_bem.py",
  "content": "\"\"\"FreeSurfer watershed BEM surfaces, driven from Windows through WSL.\n\nThe volumetric route's head model is a 3-layer BEM whose surfaces come from\nFreeSurfer's `mri_watershed` -- run in WSL because FreeSurfer is Linux-only. This\nmirrors the SimNIBS subprocess bridge: the pipeline stays in the Windows MNE\nenvironment and shells out for the one Linux tool.\n\nWatershed gives *closed, nested* inner-skull / outer-skull / outer-skin surfaces\n(the property home-grown marching-cubes could not guarantee on real data), which\nis exactly what `mne.make_bem_model` needs. Everything downstream (volume source\nspace, coregistration, forward) then lives in the FreeSurfer *conformed* MRI\nframe, so this route is self-contained and must not be mixed with a trans from\nthe SimNIBS/charm frame.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport logging\nimport shutil\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nfrom . import wsl\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\n\n# MNE BEM surface names (what make_bem_model reads from <subject>/bem/).\n_BEM_SURFACES = (\"inner_skull\", \"outer_skull\", \"outer_skin\")\n\n\nclass FreeSurferError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when a FreeSurfer step in WSL fails or produces no output.\"\"\"\n\n\ndef make_watershed_bem(\n    paths: SubjectPaths,\n    t1_nifti: str | Path,\n    *,\n    distro: str | None = None,\n    freesurfer_home: str | None = None,\n    overwrite: bool = False,\n    logger: logging.Logger | None = None,\n) -> dict[str, Path]:\n    \"\"\"Conform the T1 and extract BEM surfaces with FreeSurfer watershed.\n\n    Runs entirely in WSL, then copies the conformed ``T1.mgz`` and the three\n    BEM surfaces into the Windows FreeSurfer-style subject layout\n    (``subjects_dir/<subject>/mri`` and ``.../bem``) where MNE expects them.\n\n    Returns a dict with paths (Windows) to ``t1_mgz`` and each BEM surface.\n    \"\"\"\n    log = logger or logging.getLogger(__name__)\n    t1_nifti = Path(t1_nifti)\n    if not t1_nifti.is_file():\n        raise FreeSurferError(f\"T1 not found: {t1_nifti}\")\n\n    bem_dir = paths.fs_subject_dir / \"bem\"\n    mri_dir = paths.fs_subject_dir / \"mri\"\n    out = {\n        \"t1_mgz\": mri_dir / \"T1.mgz\",\n        **{n: bem_dir / f\"{n}.surf\" for n in _BEM_SURFACES},\n    }\n    if not overwrite and all(p.exists() for p in out.values()):\n        log.info(\"Watershed BEM already present for %s\", paths.subject)\n        return out\n\n    # Work inside the WSL home (native ext4) for speed, not on /mnt/c.\n    work = f\"$HOME/.mri2mne_fs/{paths.subject}\"\n    t1_wsl = wsl.to_wsl_path(t1_nifti)\n\n    log.info(\"FreeSurfer conform + watershed for %s (in WSL)\", paths.subject)\n    script = (\n        f\"set -e; \"\n        f\"W={work}; rm -rf $W; mkdir -p $W/mri/transforms $W/bem/watershed; \"\n        # 1. conform the T1 to FreeSurfer's 256^3 1mm space\n        f'mri_convert --conform \"{t1_wsl}\" $W/mri/T1.mgz >/dev/null 2>&1; '\n        # 2. watershed: closed, nested BEM surfaces in surface RAS\n        f\"mri_watershed -useSRAS -surf $W/bem/watershed/sub \"\n        f\"$W/mri/T1.mgz $W/bem/watershed/ws.mgz >/dev/null 2>&1; \"\n        # sanity: the four surfaces must exist\n        f\"for s in brain inner_skull outer_skull outer_skin; do \"\n        f'test -f $W/bem/watershed/sub_${{s}}_surface || '\n        f'{{ echo MISSING_$s; exit 3; }}; done; '\n        f\"echo WS_OK\"\n    )\n    res = wsl.run_freesurfer(\n        script, freesurfer_home=freesurfer_home, distro=distro,\n        check=False, timeout=1800, logger=log,\n    )\n    if \"WS_OK\" not in res.stdout:\n        raise FreeSurferError(\n            \"mri_watershed did not produce the expected surfaces.\\n\"\n            f\"stdout: {res.stdout.strip()[-500:]}\\n\"\n            f\"stderr: {res.stderr.strip()[-500:]}\"\n        )\n\n    # Copy conformed T1 + the 3 BEM surfaces back

## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T12:31:35.802Z</sub>

<details><summary>🔧 Tool call: <code>Bash</code></summary>

```json
{
  "command": "cd \"C:/Users/USER/Documents/Henri_Mondor/IRM_to_MNE_python\"; \"C:/Users/USER/Miniconda3/envs/irm2mne/python.exe\" - <<'PY' 2>&1 | tail -30\nimport sys; sys.path.insert(0,\"src\")\nfrom pathlib import Path\nimport numpy as np, mne\nfrom mne.surface import _points_outside_surface, complete_surface_info, read_surface\nmne.set_log_level(\"ERROR\")\nSP = Path(r\"C:/Users/USER/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-USER-Documents-Henri-Mondor-IRM-to-MNE-python/6c11610c-cac9-4396-8398-266fd741c3e0/scratchpad\")\n\ndef load(name, folder, pref):\n    rr, tris = read_surface(str(SP/folder/f\"{pref}{name}_surface\"))\n    s = dict(rr=rr/1000., tris=tris, ntri=len(tris), np=len(rr))\n    return complete_surface_info(s, do_neighbor_tri=False)\n\nfor folder,pref,tag in [(\"ws_surf\",\"sub_\",\"default\"),(\"ws_atlas\",\"atlas_\",\"atlas\")]:\n    inner = load(\"inner_skull\", folder, pref)\n    outer = load(\"outer_skull\", folder, pref)\n    skin  = load(\"outer_skin\", folder, pref)\n    # inner vertices outside outer_skull?\n    out_mask = _points_outside_surface(inner[\"rr\"], outer, n_jobs=1)\n    n_out = int(out_mask.sum())\n    # penetration depth (mm) for offending verts: dist to nearest outer vertex\n    if n_out:\n        from scipy.spatial import cKDTree\n        d = cKDTree(outer[\"rr\"]).query(inner[\"rr\"][out_mask])[0]*1000\n        depth = f\"max {d.max():.1f}mm, median {np.median(d):.1f}mm\"\n    else:\n        depth = \"-\"\n    # also skull thickness stats (outer-inner) via radial from centroid\n    c = inner[\"rr\"].mean(0)\n    ri = np.linalg.norm(inner[\"rr\"]-c,axis=1); ro=np.linalg.norm(outer[\"rr\"]-c,axis=1)\n    print(f\"[{tag}] inner verts OUTSIDE outer: {n_out}/{inner['np']}  penetration {depth}\")\n    print(f\"        inner r: {ri.min()*1000:.0f}-{ri.max()*1000:.0f}mm  outer r: {ro.min()*1000:.0f}-{ro.max()*1000:.0f}mm\")\nPY",
  "description": "Measure inner/outer skull interpenetration magnitude"
}

- Seulement **72/10242 sommets** (0,7 %) d'inner_skull sortent d'outer_skull, pénétration max **7,5 mm**.
- MAIS les rayons partent en vrille : outer_skull jusqu'à **161 mm**, inner jusqu'à 108 mm (une tête normale ≈ 90-100 mm). Il y a des **pics/aberrations** dans les surfaces de crâne.

**Cause** : ton T1 est un acquisition clinique **grand FOV** (0,7 mm, 274×384×384) qui inclut le cou/les épaules. `mri_watershed` sur un T1 brut non normalisé happe des tissus non-crâniens à la base → pics → intersections. Le `-atlas` n'y change rien (même résultat).

C'est le **talon d'Achille classique du watershed sur T1 brut**, et ça ne se règle pas proprement au cas par cas (whack-a-mole). La vraie solution standard change un peu la donne — d'où ma question avant de continuer :

1	"""Coregistration inputs from the SimNIBS head model (pipeline side).

4	scalp surface and subject-space fiducials out of the head model, then writes
5	them as the FreeSurfer-style files MNE's coregistration expects. Everything is
6	in mesh-world coordinates, which SimNIBS treats as MNE's "MRI" frame.
7	"""

9	from __future__ import annotations

11	import json
12	import logging

25	    logger: logging.Logger,
26	    timeout_s: int = 1800,
27	) -> dict[str, list[float]]:

29	
30	    Returns the fiducial dictionary (mesh-world mm). The head surface is the
31	    SimNIBS skin surface; the fiducials are the ones charm warped into subject
32	    space. Reuses MNE's coregistration downstream, unchanged.

40	    fids_json = paths.root / "coreg" / "fiducials.json"

Distinct from the SimNIBS FEM surface route -- never mix a charm-frame\ntrans with these.\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport logging\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport numpy as np\n\nfrom .paths import SubjectPaths\n\n\nclass VolumetricError(RuntimeError):\n    \"\"\"Raised when a volumetric BEM step cannot proceed.\"\"\"\n\n\ndef prepare_coreg_inputs(paths: SubjectPaths, logger: logging.Logger) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Stage the shared coregistration inputs in the FreeSurfer MRI frame.\n\n    Writes bem/<subject>-head.fif (the watershed outer-skin scalp) and\n    bem/<subject>-fiducials.fif (MNI fiducials mapped through the subject's\n    Talairach transform), after which `coregistration.fit_coregistration` runs\n    unchanged -- the very same path the SimNIBS route uses.\n    \"\"\"\n    import mne\n    from mne.coreg import get_mni_fiducials\n    from mne.io.constants import FIFF\n    from mne.surface import complete_surface_info, read_surface\n\n    outer_skin = paths.fs_subject_dir / \"bem\" / \"outer_skin.surf\"\n    if not outer_skin.is_file():\n        raise VolumetricError(\n            f\"Missing {outer_skin}. | Module | Rôle |
|---|---|
| `wsl.py` | pont WSL (chemins, exécuteur, source FreeSurfer, probe) — testé |
| `freesurfer_bem.py` | `build_bem_anatomy()` : autorecon1 + watershed en WSL → surfaces propres + T1.mgz + talairach + orig.mgz rapatriés |
| `volumetric.py` | côté MNE : `prepare_coreg_inputs` (scalp + fiduciaux MNI), `make_bem`, `setup_volume_source`, `make_volume_forward`, `make_volume_inverse`, `apply_volume_inverse` (→ `-vl.stc`), `peak_location_volume` |

La coregistration est **partagée** avec la route surfacique (même `fit_coregistration`, mêmes fichiers `-head.fif`/`-fiducials.fif`) — j'ai réutilisé exactement ta convention.

**Dès qu'autorecon1 finit** (je serai notifié, ~20-30 min), je lance le **dé-risque volumique complet** : watershed sur la sortie normalisée → BEM 3 couches → source space volumique → coreg → forward → inverse dSPM volumique, avec l'EEG sample comme entrée fonctionnelle (validation de plomberie, comme pour le surfacique). ## 🤖 Assistant  <sub>2026-07-22T12:44:45.572Z</sub>

Bonne question — c'est un terme que j'emploie beaucoup, autant le clarifier.

**« Dé-risquer » = tester en isolation l'étape la plus incertaine d'un plan, sur des données réelles, *avant* d'écrire tout le code propre autour.** L'idée : au lieu de construire les 600 lignes du module complet puis de découvrir à la fin que la brique centrale ne marche pas (et ne plus savoir *laquelle* des 600 lignes est en cause), je prends d'abord LE point qui pourrait tout faire capoter, je le lance seul en quelques commandes, et je vérifie qu'il produit ce qu'il faut.

C'est une façon de **faire échouer vite et pas cher** la partie risquée, plutôt que tard et cher.

Concrètement dans ce projet, à chaque étape j'ai isolé l'inconnue :

| Étape | Le « risque » (l'inconnue) | Le dé-risque (test isolé) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward FEM (surfacique) | SimNIBS extrait-il un leadfield exploitable par MNE ? | script `spike_fem.py` sur une sortie charm réelle | ✅ ok → j'ai codé le module |
| Route C, watershed | FreeSurfer produit-il des surfaces BEM **fermées** (ce que le marching-cubes ratait) ? | `mri_watershed` seul sur ton vrai T1 + vérif MNE (Euler=2) | ✅ fermées, mais... |
| Route C, qualité crâne | ...emboîtées ? | mesure des intersections | ❌ 72 pics → d'où le choix `autorecon1` |

Le dernier point illustre bien l'intérêt : le dé-risque a **révélé le problème de crâne bruité en 2 min**, ce qui nous a fait choisir `autorecon1` **avant** que j'aie écrit et débogué tout le forward/inverse volumique par-dessus des surfaces inutilisables.

Donc quand je dis « je lance le dé-risque volumique complet dès qu'autorecon1 finit », je veux dire : **exécuter la chaîne entière une fois, à la main, sur ton vrai T1 + l'EEG sample**, pour confirmer que chaque maillon s'enchaîne (BEM → source space → coreg → forward → inverse → `.stc`) et donne une sortie cohérente. Si un maillon casse, je le vois isolément et je corrige *ça*, sans avoir à démêler tout le pipeline. Une fois ce passage validé, j'emballe proprement dans le wrapper + les tests + le README, avec la certitude que le fond fonctionne.

C'est exactement l'esprit de la mémoire projet que j'avais notée : *« benchmark BLAS before blaming the algorithm »* — vérifier la brique de base avant de bâtir dessus.
